1991.assessment:
Post-1990 democracy transition. Nepal's fiscal situation weak with
high aid dependence. Budget deficit manageable through foreign aid
and Indian support. Debt moderate for LDC. New democratic government
inheriting weak fiscal structure from Panchayat system.

1992.assessment:
Fiscal situation gradually stabilizing. Multiple coalition governments
creating fiscal uncertainty. Foreign aid (India, World Bank, ADB)
dominant in development budget. Revenue mobilization weak.

1993.assessment:
Fiscal management improving with democratic government. Revenue
base limited. Foreign aid dominates capital budget. Recurring
government instability hindering fiscal reform.

1994.assessment:
Fiscal situation stable at weak level. Coalition government instability.
Nepal relying on donor support for development spending. Revenue
collection improving slowly.

1995.assessment:
Stable but weak fiscal position. CPN-M launched insurgency February
1996. Year before insurgency — fiscal baseline. Development budget
dependent on donors. Tax revenue growing slowly.

1996.assessment:
Insurgency beginning to affect fiscal position. Security spending
increasing. Revenue collection disrupted in insurgency-affected
districts. Development budget declining. Fiscal deterioration beginning.

1997.assessment:
Security spending increasing significantly. Insurgency expanding.
Revenue collection declining in conflict areas. Development spending
being displaced by security expenditure. Fiscal pressures rising.

1998.assessment:
Fiscal deterioration from insurgency impacts. Security budget growing.
Revenue shortfalls. IMF stabilization program discussions. Donors
concerned about fiscal sustainability with conflict.

1999.assessment:
Fiscal situation strained by insurgency. Security spending consuming
growing share of budget. Revenue collection difficult in Maoist-controlled
areas. Development budget squeezed. Foreign aid maintaining fiscal viability.

2000.assessment:
Fiscal pressure from insurgency peak. Security budget consuming 15%+
of total expenditure. Revenue declining in conflict districts. Multiple
PM changes undermining fiscal continuity. Foreign aid essential for
development.

2001.assessment:
Royal massacre and intensified insurgency further strained fiscal
position. Security spending sharply higher. State of emergency cost.
IMF emergency support. Revenue collection difficult. Fiscal deterioration.

2002.assessment:
Fiscal situation highly stressed. Security budget near 20% of total
spending. Revenue collection across Nepal severely impacted. Insurgency
at peak intensity. Emergency rule. Donor support maintaining government.

2003.assessment:
Continued fiscal stress from insurgency. Ceasefire attempts did not
significantly relieve fiscal pressure. IMF structural adjustment
conditions. Revenue mobilization improving somewhat with electronic
tax collection.

2004.assessment:
Fiscal stress continuing. King Gyanendra dismissing governments.
Security spending high. Revenue collection growing slowly. Development
budget squeezed. Donor support critical. IMF program ongoing.

2005.assessment:
Royal coup February 2005. Fiscal situation strained. Security spending
high. Donors suspended some assistance due to royal takeover. Remittances
growing providing private sector cushion. IMF relations strained.

2006.assessment:
Peace agreement in November 2006 — major improvement in fiscal outlook.
Security spending declining trajectory. Donors resuming support. Post-peace
dividend in fiscal space. Revenue mobilization improving with peace.

2007.assessment:
Post-peace fiscal improvement. Security spending declining. Donors
fully resuming assistance. Revenue collection improving with peace.
Political transition to republic underway. Fiscal space improving.

2008.assessment:
Republic declared. Post-conflict fiscal recovery continuing. Development
budget expanding with donor support. Revenue mobilization improving.
GDP growing strongly. Fiscal position improving with peace dividend.

2009.assessment:
Fiscal management improving but political instability (Prachanda
resignation) creating uncertainty. Revenue collections growing with
economic growth. Remittances providing strong indirect fiscal support
through reduced social spending pressure.

2010.assessment:
Fiscal situation manageable. Government revenues growing with economic
growth. Multiple PM changes creating budget uncertainty. IMF assessed
fiscal position as manageable with reform progress.

2011.assessment:
Fiscal position manageable. Revenue growing. CA expired without constitution.
Political deadlock not severely impacting fiscal management. Remittances
over 20% GDP providing strong external support.

2012.assessment:
Fiscal position stable. CA dissolved May 2012. Government in caretaker
mode but fiscal management continued. Revenue growing with economic
activity. Debt levels manageable.

2013.assessment:
Manageable fiscal position. Second CA elected. Revenue mobilization
improving. Nepal's public debt around 30-40% GDP — low for development
country. IMF program supportive.

2014.assessment:
Fiscal position broadly manageable. Revenue growing with economy.
Political instability but fiscal management maintained. Nepal's fiscal
situation relatively stable for LDC at this stage.

2015.assessment:
Earthquake (April 2015) and India blockade (September-November 2015)
severely impacted fiscal situation. Emergency reconstruction spending
required. Revenue declined. India blockade disrupted all supply chains.
International aid partially offset fiscal shock. Constitution promulgated.

2016.assessment:
Post-earthquake reconstruction ongoing. India blockade ended. Fiscal
normalization. International reconstruction aid supporting budget.
Fiscal position recovering. PM changes continuing but fiscal management
maintained.

2017.assessment:
Fiscal position improving with strong economic growth. Federal elections.
First elections under new constitution. Revenue growing significantly.
Public debt declining as percentage of GDP. Fiscal management improving.

2018.assessment:
Strong fiscal position for Nepal LDC. Revenue growing. NCP government
with strong majority promising political stability. Debt manageable.
Remittances and economic growth supporting fiscal health.

2019.assessment:
Manageable fiscal position. Revenue growing. Some fiscal expansion
for capital projects (hydropower, roads). Nepal fiscal situation
strongest in decades. IMF assessed favorably.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 impacted fiscal position. Revenue declined with economic
contraction. Emergency health spending required. Nepal received
emergency IMF support. Political crisis — Oli dissolved parliament.
Fiscal position manageable with IMF support but strained.

2021.assessment:
Fiscal recovery beginning. Revenue recovering with economic growth.
Political crisis resolved (parliament restored by Supreme Court). Post-COVID
fiscal rebuilding. Debt manageable. IMF assessed Nepal as on recovery path.

2022.assessment:
Fiscal stress from import surge and FX reserve depletion. Government
imposed import restrictions to protect reserves. Revenue challenged.
Nepal's FX reserves under pressure from import surge. Elections
November 2022. Fiscal management under strain.

2023.assessment:
Fiscal recovery with normalization of import restrictions. FX reserves
recovering. Revenue growing. Prachanda PM. Coalition government.
Debt still manageable. IMF Article IV noted improving fiscal trajectory.

2024.assessment:
Manageable fiscal position. Oli returned as PM July 2024 in coalition
with NC. Revenue growing. FX reserves recovered. Debt levels manageable.
Nepal's fiscal situation broadly stable at LDC level.

1991.assessment:
Nepal GDP per capita $195.72 in 1991. Political transition from absolute
monarchy to constitutional monarchy with multiparty democracy (1990
People's Movement). Economy predominantly agricultural, severely poor.
Among the world's lowest GDPs per capita. Baseline for democratic transition.

1992.assessment:
GDP per capita declined to $165.03 due to currency adjustments and
economic disruption of transition period. High inflation (17%) eroding
real incomes. Nepal's post-democracy transition period.

1993.assessment:
Modest recovery. Political instability with coalition governments but
economy slowly growing. Remittances beginning to play larger role.

1994.assessment:
Continued recovery. Tourism and carpets as export sectors. Hydropower
potential being developed slowly. Political instability with multiple
governments not helping growth.

1995.assessment:
GDP recovering toward 1991 levels. CPN-Maoist launched People's War in
February 1996. Pre-insurgency economic baseline. Tourism growth. Remittances
expanding. Agriculture dominant.

1996.assessment:
Maoist insurgency launched February 1996. Initial impact limited as
fighting confined to remote western districts. GDP maintained. Tourism
sector beginning to feel some impact. Insurgency began but economy
not yet significantly affected.

1997.assessment:
GDP grew despite ongoing low-level insurgency. Insurgency expanding to
more districts. Economy growing through tourism, carpet exports, remittances.
Regional neighbors (India) maintained normal trade.

1998.assessment:
Modest decline in USD terms (partly India-linked currency). Insurgency
intensifying in mid-hills and western districts. Tourism beginning to
decline. Economy resilient due to subsistence agriculture base.

1999.assessment:
Continuing growth despite insurgency. Remittances from Gulf countries
and India growing significantly. Garment, carpet exports. Insurgency
escalating but economy maintaining resilience through remittances.

2000.assessment:
GDP growing despite intensifying insurgency. Insurgency escalating
significantly in 2000 with Maoists attacking police posts. Tourism
declining. Remittances offsetting conflict impacts. Garment sector
growing. Economy resilient.

2001.assessment:
Royal massacre June 1, 2001 killed King Birendra and family. King
Gyanendra took throne. Maoists declared truce briefly. Tourism collapsed
after royal massacre and 9/11 global tourism decline. GDP still growing
in USD terms partly due to exchange rate effects.

2002.assessment:
GDP nearly flat. King Gyanendra dismissed government October 2002.
Insurgency at high intensity. Emergency declared. Tourism near collapse.
Garment exports declining. Remittances sustaining economy. Civil war
period but subsistence agriculture kept GDP stable.

2003.assessment:
Modest GDP growth despite ongoing insurgency. Ceasefire attempts in 2003.
Some tourism recovery. Remittances growing strongly. Hydropower projects
continuing. Conflict impacts offset by remittance economy.

2004.assessment:
Growing GDP despite intense insurgency. King Gyanendra taking direct
control. Insurgency widespread nationally. But remittances growing
strongly from Gulf and India labor migration offsetting domestic conflict.

2005.assessment:
King Gyanendra seized direct power February 2005. Royal coup. Insurgency
ongoing. Despite political crisis, GDP growing due to resilient remittance
economy. Economy partially insulated from conflict by high remittances.

2006.assessment:
April 2006 People's Movement (Jana Andolan II) forced King to reinstate
parliament. Comprehensive Peace Agreement signed November 2006 ended
Maoist insurgency. Post-peace dividend. GDP growing. Regime change
toward federalism beginning.

2007.assessment:
Post-peace GDP growing strongly. Political transition toward republic
underway. Interim constitution adopted. Constituent Assembly elections
prepared. Peace dividend boosting confidence. Remittances and some
tourism recovery driving growth.

2008.assessment:
Constituent Assembly elected April 2008; Maoists won largest share.
Nepal declared federal democratic republic in May 2008 — abolishing
monarchy. Second regime change. GDP growing strongly. Remittances booming.

2009.assessment:
Prachanda resigned as PM in May 2009 (dispute over army chief). Political
instability with multiple PM changes. GDP continuing to grow through
remittances. Political dysfunction not significantly impacting economic
growth.

2010.assessment:
Strong GDP growth. Remittances from Gulf and Malaysia labor migration
growing rapidly. Constitution writing deadlocked in Constituent Assembly.
Multiple PM changes. Economy growing despite political dysfunction.

2011.assessment:
Strong GDP growth. Remittances reaching 20%+ of GDP. Constituent Assembly
term extended twice; failed to write constitution. Political deadlock
continuing but economy growing. CA dissolved without completing constitution.

2012.assessment:
GDP near flat (currency effects). Constituent Assembly dissolved May 2012
without constitution. Political vacuum. Economy continued growing through
remittances. New Constituent Assembly elections planned.

2013.assessment:
Second Constituent Assembly elected November 2013. GDP growing. Remittances
over 25% of GDP. Political transition continuing. NC-led government working
on constitution. Economic growth driven by services and remittances.

2014.assessment:
Continued growth. Constitution drafting proceeding. Nepal growing as
lower-middle income country with remittance-driven economy. Tourism
recovering. Moderate growth trajectory.

2015.assessment:
April 25 earthquake (magnitude 7.8) killed ~9,000, destroyed infrastructure,
displaced 3 million. Economic impact catastrophic with reconstruction costs
estimated at $7 billion (more than one year's GDP). New constitution
promulgated September 2015 despite protests. GDP maintained only through
remittances and aid. Real growth contracted.

2016.assessment:
Post-earthquake reconstruction. India blockade ended (continued from
late 2015). Real growth recovering. Continued political instability with
PM changes. Remittances maintained. Gradual recovery from earthquake impacts.

2017.assessment:
Nepal crossed $1,000 GDP per capita milestone. New federal constitution
elections held in November-December 2017 — first under new constitution.
Left alliance (NC-CPN-UML) won majority. Strong GDP growth. Remittances
driving income growth. Nepal approaching lower-middle income threshold.

2018.assessment:
Strong growth continuing. KP Sharma Oli became PM. First government
under new federal constitution. Nepal Communist Party formed from merger.
Remittances over $8 billion (26% GDP). GDP per capita growing rapidly.

2019.assessment:
Continued growth. Nepal becoming lower-middle income country. Tourism
growing (Visit Nepal Year 2020 planned). Remittances strong. Political
tensions within NCP. Hydropower development accelerating.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 caused tourism collapse and remittance decline. GDP declined
3.4% in real terms. Visit Nepal Year 2020 canceled. Migrant workers
returned home temporarily. Political crisis — Oli dissolved parliament
twice (ruled unconstitutional by Supreme Court).

2021.assessment:
Recovery from COVID-19 impacts. NCP split; Sher Bahadur Deuba became
PM after Supreme Court restored parliament. Remittances recovered.
GDP recovering. Political instability high but economy resilient.

2022.assessment:
Strong recovery. November 2022 elections. Deuba (NC) lost to Prachanda
coalition. Nepal approaching $1,400 GDP per capita. Remittances near
record levels. Growing tourism. Nepal's economy resilient despite
political instability.

2023.assessment:
Slight GDP decline in USD terms (currency effects). Prachanda PM continued.
Coalition government shifts. Nepal maintaining growth trajectory. Remittances
strong. Hydropower generation increasing.

2024.assessment:
Nepal at highest ever GDP per capita. KP Sharma Oli returned as PM (July
2024) through new coalition with NC. Economy growing modestly. Remittances
dominant income source. Nepal at lower-middle income level.

1991.assessment:
High inflation 15.56% in transition year. 1990-91 political movement
and transition to multiparty democracy created some economic disruption.
Nepal's inflation closely linked to India given open border.

1992.assessment:
High inflation continuing at 17.15%. Post-transition economic adjustment.
India-Nepal monetary linkage transmitting inflation pressures. Supply chain
disruptions from political transition period.

1993.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 7.51%. Monetary stabilization after transition.
Nepal Rastra Bank maintaining moderate monetary policy. India's lower
inflation in this period helpful.

1994.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 8.35%. Political instability (coalition governments)
but monetary management stable. Food price pressures from agricultural
variations.

1995.assessment:
Moderate inflation continuing. Year before Maoist insurgency launched.
Food prices driver. Monetary conditions stable following NRB management.

1996.assessment:
Inflation at 9.22% in first year of Maoist insurgency. Supply disruptions
in some districts but overall price stability maintained. India-linked
monetary dynamics dominant.

1997.assessment:
Low inflation at 4.01%. Good agricultural year. India's low inflation
transmitted through peg. Insurgency ongoing but not significantly
affecting national price levels.

1998.assessment:
Inflation spiked to 11.24%. India's high inflation (Asian crisis context)
transmitted to Nepal. Food price shocks. Insurgency intensifying in
western districts. Combined pressures on prices.

1999.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 7.45%. Insurgency intensifying but food prices
moderated. NRB monetary management maintained stability despite conflict.

2000.assessment:
Low inflation at 2.48%. Good agricultural output. India's moderate
inflation keeping Nepal's prices in check despite insurgency. Supply
disruptions limited in major urban areas.

2001.assessment:
Low inflation despite royal massacre and intensifying insurgency. Economy
resilient. Monetary stability maintained. Royal massacre political crisis
not generating price instability.

2002.assessment:
Low inflation at 3.03%. Government dismissed by king; emergency declared.
State of emergency and curfews creating some supply disruptions in urban
areas but national inflation remained moderate.

2003.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 5.71%. Insurgency at near-peak intensity. Supply
disruptions more widespread. Oil price increases. Failed ceasefire in 2003.
Prices rising moderately.

2004.assessment:
Low inflation at 2.84%. Good agricultural year. Remittances helping
maintain household purchasing power despite insurgency. Monetary stability.

2005.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 6.84%. Royal coup February 2005. Insurgency
ongoing. Oil price increases globally feeding through. India's rising
inflation transmitted. Supply disruptions in some areas.

2006.assessment:
Moderate inflation in year of peace agreement. Jana Andolan II successful
in April; Comprehensive Peace Agreement November. Post-peace transition.
Inflation at 6.92% moderate. Oil price pressure continuing.

2007.assessment:
Low inflation at 2.27% in post-peace transition year. Good agricultural
season. Peace dividends beginning. India's low inflation transmitted.
Political transition to republic underway.

2008.assessment:
Inflation spiked to 9.91% from global food and fuel price crisis. Republic
declared May 2008. Global commodity price shock in 2008 driving food and
fuel costs. India's 9% inflation transmitted to Nepal.

2009.assessment:
Elevated inflation at 11.09%. Food prices continuing high. India's
inflation (around 11%) transmitted through Nepal-India monetary linkage.
Prachanda resigned. Multiple PM changes. Political instability not
causing additional price instability beyond India transmission.

2010.assessment:
Continued elevated inflation at 9.33%. Food prices elevated globally.
India's continued high inflation transmitted. Remittances helping household
incomes but national price level high.

2011.assessment:
High inflation persisting at 9.23%. India's elevated inflation continuing
to transmit to Nepal. Food and fuel prices high. Constituent Assembly
term expired without constitution.

2012.assessment:
Elevated inflation continuing at 9.46%. CA dissolved without constitution.
Political vacuum. India's high inflation still transmitting. Food prices
elevated. Economy growing through remittances despite political deadlock.

2013.assessment:
Inflation stable at elevated level (9.04%). Second CA elected. Political
transition moving. India's inflation declining from 2011-12 peak, beginning
to help Nepal's price stability.

2014.assessment:
Inflation declining to 8.36%. Oil price decline globally beginning to
reduce Nepal's fuel costs. India's inflation declining. Remittances
maintaining household purchasing power.

2015.assessment:
Inflation at 7.87% despite April earthquake and India blockade (later
2015). Earthquake reconstruction spending and Indian blockade caused
acute supply shortages and price spikes for fuel and basic goods in
late 2015. Annual average still moderate.

2016.assessment:
Elevated inflation at 8.79% with India blockade's delayed effects in
early 2016. Blockade ended late 2015/early 2016. Supply normalizing.
India's declining inflation beginning to transmit favorably.

2017.assessment:
Inflation dropped sharply to 2.78%. India's very low inflation (3-4%)
transmitted to Nepal. Good agricultural year. Post-blockade supply
normalization. First federal elections in November. Monetary stability.

2018.assessment:
Low-moderate inflation at 4.41%. Oil price recovery slightly increasing
fuel costs. India's moderate inflation transmitted. Economy growing well.
Good monetary management under Nepal Rastra Bank.

2019.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 5.57%. Economy growing. Food and fuel prices
stable. India's moderate inflation levels transmitted. Housing costs
growing with urbanization. Monetary conditions stable.

2020.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 5.06% despite COVID-19. Supply chain disruptions
from lockdowns raised some prices but oil price collapse offset fuel
inflation. Economy contracted in real terms. NRB maintained monetary
stability.

2021.assessment:
Low inflation at 4.13%. Post-COVID recovery beginning. Remittances
recovering. Global inflation only beginning to build in late 2021.
NRB maintained monetary stability through political turbulence.

2022.assessment:
Elevated inflation at 7.67% from global commodity price surge. Russia-
Ukraine war driving food and fuel price rises. India's inflation transmitted.
NRB tightened monetary policy. Elections in November 2022. Inflation
becoming significant political issue.

2023.assessment:
Inflation declining but still elevated at 7.12%. Global commodity prices
moderating. NRB tight monetary policy working. Coalition politics with
Prachanda as PM. Economy stabilizing.

2024.assessment:
Inflation declining to 4.69%. Global commodity price moderation.
NRB monetary tightening working. KP Sharma Oli returned as PM July 2024.
Economy stabilizing with moderate growth and moderate inflation.

1991.assessment:
Nepal trade at 34.68% of GDP in 1991. Heavily dependent on India for
imports and exports. Post-democracy transition. Open border with India
dominates trade. Carpet and garment exports growing.

1992.assessment:
Trade openness at 41.70%. Carpet and garment exports booming.
Nepal benefiting from GSP preferences and garment quotas. High
trade-to-GDP ratio for landlocked LDC.

1993.assessment:
High trade openness at 47%. Nepal exporting garments, carpets, and
importing consumer goods and fuel. India trade dominant.

1994.assessment:
Trade at 50% of GDP — high for LDC. Nepal's open border with India
and trade liberalization producing high trade-to-GDP ratio. Garments
and carpets dominant exports.

1995.assessment:
High trade openness at 59.49%. Nepal's economy increasingly integrated
with Indian and global markets. Garment exports near peak under
multi-fiber arrangement. Trade liberalization progressing.

1996.assessment:
High trade openness continuing at 58%. Maoist insurgency launched but
not affecting national trade significantly in first year. Landlocked
position with India transit dominance.

1997.assessment:
Trade at 64% — highest level in early period. Nepal's garment exports
very strong before end of Multi-Fiber Arrangement. India trade dominant
with also Tibet/China trade growing.

1998.assessment:
Trade declining from peak. Asian financial crisis reducing regional
trade. Garment exports declining. Insurgency expanding in western
districts affecting some trade routes.

1999.assessment:
Trade at 52.57%. Garment exports declining from MFA peak. Insurgency
intensifying but not yet significantly impacting national trade.
India-Nepal trade dominant. Tourism sector contributing.

2000.assessment:
Trade at 55.71%. Economy maintaining trade openness despite insurgency.
Remittances growing as major external income source. India trade dominant.

2001.assessment:
Trade at 55.80%. Royal massacre and 9/11 affected tourism. Insurgency
intensifying. Trade maintained through India open border despite crisis.

2002.assessment:
Trade declining to 46.23%. Insurgency causing supply chain disruptions.
Tourism collapsed. Garment exports declining with MFA phase-out approaching.
State of emergency.

2003.assessment:
Trade at 44.25%. Declining from peak as insurgency and MFA expiry impact
exports. Some ceasefire attempts. India trade still dominant. Service
exports (tourism, IT) very limited.

2004.assessment:
Trade at 46.15%. Economy continuing despite insurgency peak. India trade
dominant. Imports of fuel, food, manufactured goods continuing through
India despite conflict.

2005.assessment:
Trade at 44%. Royal coup. Insurgency ongoing. Garment exports collapsed
with 2005 MFA expiry. New trade structure emerging with remittances
replacing garment export income.

2006.assessment:
Trade at 44.76%. Peace agreement year. Post-insurgency trade recovery
anticipated. India trade dominant. Service trade (tourism) beginning
recovery. Garment exports gone; import-driven trade dominant.

2007.assessment:
Trade stable at 44.58%. Post-peace transition. Tourism recovering.
Imports growing with improved consumer spending from remittances.
India trade dominant. Trade structure now heavily import-dependent.

2008.assessment:
Trade at 46%. Republic declared. Tourism growing (Everest industry).
Oil price spike increasing import costs. India trade dominant. Nepal
increasingly import-dependent with remittances financing trade deficit.

2009.assessment:
Trade at 47%. Political instability with PM changes not affecting trade.
India open border and trade agreement dominant. Growing import trade
financed by remittances. Nepal trade deficit widening.

2010.assessment:
Trade at 46%. Remittances over 15% GDP. Import-led trade growth.
Nepal's trade structure heavily India-dependent. China trade growing
slowly. Multiple PM changes not affecting trade.

2011.assessment:
Trade declined to 36.30% — sharp drop due to GDP growth outpacing trade
growth (denominator effect with rapid GDP growth in USD from currency).
Nepal's economy growing fast. Trade still primarily India-dependent.

2012.assessment:
Trade recovering at 38%. CA dissolved. Political deadlock. Trade maintained
through India open border arrangement. Remittances financing trade deficit.

2013.assessment:
Trade recovering to 41.87%. Second CA elected. Economic growth
moderate. Trade structure import-dominated with India supplying
80%+ of imports.

2014.assessment:
Trade growing at 46%. Nepal economy growing. Imports from India
and China growing. Remittances at $6 billion+ financing trade deficit.
China trade corridor being developed.

2015.assessment:
Trade at 46.67%. Earthquake significantly disrupted supply chains
in April-May 2015. India blockade from September 2015 caused acute
fuel and goods shortage. Annual figure masks severe H2 disruptions.
Reconstruction imports boosted trade figures.

2016.assessment:
Trade at 42.12%. Post-blockade normalization. India blockade ended
early 2016. Trade recovering. Reconstruction imports declining as
immediate post-earthquake needs met.

2017.assessment:
Trade growing at 44.64%. Economic recovery continuing. Federal elections.
China trade growing slightly. India dominant. Import-led economy with
remittance income financing trade deficit.

2018.assessment:
Trade at 48.45%. Growing imports of vehicles, electronics. Remittances
over $8 billion. Trade deficit widening. Nepal's economy growing but
import-dependent structure unchanged. India trade 65%+ of total.

2019.assessment:
Trade near 50% of GDP. Growing import economy. Nepal preparing for
Visit Nepal Year 2020 (tourism). Trade deficit significant.
Remittances and tourism key foreign exchange earners. India trade dominant.

2020.assessment:
Trade declined to 40.92% with COVID-19 lockdowns. Tourism service
exports collapsed. Import reduction from lockdowns. Trade recovery
expected with vaccine rollout.

2021.assessment:
Trade recovering at 43%. Post-COVID reopening. Imports recovering.
Tourism still limited. Political crisis — parliamentary dissolution and
restoration. Economy recovering despite political turbulence.

2022.assessment:
Trade recovering to 49%. Post-COVID import surge. Russia-Ukraine war
increased fuel import costs. Nepal's trade deficit widening significantly.
Foreign exchange reserves under pressure. Elections November 2022.

2023.assessment:
Trade declining to 41.57%. Import restrictions imposed by Nepal
government to protect FX reserves (luxury goods). NRB tightening.
Trade deficit narrowing. FX reserves recovering.

2024.assessment:
Trade at 40.53%. Import restrictions eased somewhat with FX recovery.
Nepal maintaining moderate trade openness. India-Nepal trade treaty
dominant framework. Oli returned as PM. Economy stable.

1991.assessment:
ILO modeled unemployment estimate ~10.5%. Nepal's unemployment statistics
are highly problematic — the country has massive underemployment and
disguised unemployment in subsistence agriculture. High formal unemployment
reflects structural labor market issues with large informal sector.
Post-1990 democracy transition.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1992.assessment:
Unemployment estimate stable at ~10.5%. Labor emigration to India
and Gulf growing. Large informal agricultural sector dominates. ILO
modeled estimates not reflecting true labor market conditions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1993.assessment:
Unemployment stable. Nepal's labor market characterized by large-scale
emigration to India (open border) and emerging Gulf migration. Official
unemployment figures not capturing labor realities.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1994.assessment:
Stable unemployment estimate. Nepal's labor market structure unchanged.
Growing migration to Gulf countries (Malaysia, Kuwait, UAE) emerging.
Large agricultural informal sector absorbing rural labor.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1995.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Year before Maoist insurgency launched. Nepal's
chronic unemployment and underemployment were contributing factors to
insurgency's appeal in poor rural areas.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1996.assessment:
Unemployment stable at chronic level. Maoist insurgency launched.
Rural underemployment and landlessness were drivers of insurgency
support. ILO modeled figures not capturing conflict-driven displacement.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1997.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Insurgency expanding. Some tourism sector impacts
in conflict areas. ILO modeled estimates essentially flat throughout
the period, masking conflict impacts on labor markets.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1998.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Insurgency intensifying. Labor emigration to India
and Gulf absorbing some unemployed. Tourism declining in conflict-affected
areas.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1999.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Insurgency at medium intensity. Malaysia labor
migration growing as major destination alongside Gulf. Remittances
important income source replacing domestic employment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2000.assessment:
Unemployment stable at structural level. Insurgency intensifying.
Tourism sector employment hit significantly. Labor emigration
accelerating as domestic conflict displaces rural workers.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2001.assessment:
Unemployment stable despite royal massacre and 9/11 tourism collapse.
ILO modeled estimates not capturing tourism employment losses. Labor
emigration absorbing unemployed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2002.assessment:
Stable unemployment. State of emergency. Insurgency near peak.
Many rural workers displaced to cities or emigrated. ILO estimates
masking conflict impacts on rural labor markets.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2003.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Ceasefire attempts in 2003. Labor emigration to
Gulf and Malaysia serving as unemployment safety valve. Remittances
growing rapidly as share of household income.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2004.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Insurgency at peak. Labor emigration absorbing
rural unemployed. GDP growing despite unemployment. Structural labor
market issues not resolved by growth.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2005.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Royal coup. Insurgency ongoing. Labor emigration
continued as primary coping strategy for unemployment. Political crisis
not affecting labor emigration safety valve.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2006.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Peace agreement year. Some tourism recovery anticipated.
Post-peace economic optimism. Labor emigration continuing as structural
factor. ILO estimates essentially unchanged throughout the period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2007.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Post-peace transition. Tourism recovering. Political
transition to republic underway. Labor market structure unchanged with
large emigration and informal sector.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2008.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Republic declared May 2008. Maoists entered
government. Some reduction in conflict-related displacement. GDP growing.
Labor emigration accelerating with remittances exceeding $2 billion.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2009.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Global financial crisis caused some reduction
in Gulf remittances temporarily. Labor emigration absorbed domestic
unemployment. Political instability with PM changes.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2010.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Remittances over 15% of GDP. Labor emigration
to Malaysia and Gulf dominant. Constitution deadlock. ILO estimates
unchanged reflecting structural nature of unemployment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2011.assessment:
Stable unemployment. CA dissolved without constitution. Remittances
growing strongly. Youth unemployment and underemployment high. Labor
emigration safety valve preventing social unrest from unemployment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2012.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Political vacuum after CA dissolution. Economy
growing through remittances. Structural unemployment unchanged. An
estimated 1,500 people per day emigrating for work.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2013.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Second CA elected. Labor emigration from Nepal
estimated at 1,500-2,000 daily (GoN figures). Remittances over 25% GDP.
Economy heavily dependent on remittances masking unemployment issues.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2014.assessment:
Stable structural unemployment. Constitution drafting continuing.
Qatar World Cup construction sites employing many Nepali workers.
Workers dying in Gulf documented (kafala system). Labor emigration
a national concern.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2015.assessment:
Earthquake and India blockade worsened actual unemployment sharply.
Construction workers displaced. Remittances initially fell as emigrants
returned. ILO modeled estimates do not capture earthquake employment shock.
Real unemployment spike in 2015 not reflected in ILO figures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2016.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Post-earthquake recovery and reconstruction
creating some employment. Remittances recovering. India blockade ended.
Labor emigration resuming to Gulf and Malaysia.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2017.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Federal elections held. Reconstruction employment
creating some jobs. Remittances near record. Labor emigration stable.
ILO estimates essentially unchanged for entire period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2018.assessment:
Stable structural unemployment. New communist-led government (NCP).
Economy growing. Labor emigration as structural feature. Unemployment
not changing significantly despite economic growth.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2019.assessment:
Stable unemployment. Tourism growing. Hydropower creating some jobs.
Labor emigration continuing at high rates. Remittances over $8 billion.
ILO estimates unchanging.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 caused significant unemployment spike to 13%. Tourism completely
closed. Returning migrants from Gulf swelled domestic labor supply.
GDP contracted. Real unemployment likely higher than ILO estimates.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2021.assessment:
Elevated unemployment at 12.24%. Partial COVID recovery. Tourism
beginning to return. Some emigrants re-emigrating. Political crisis
with parliamentary dissolution. Unemployment declining from COVID peak.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2022.assessment:
Unemployment returning to structural level. Post-COVID recovery. Tourism
recovering. Labor emigration resuming. Elections November 2022. Economy
recovering but structural unemployment unchanged.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2023.assessment:
Stable structural unemployment. Nepal GDP growing. Remittances strong.
Labor emigration continuing at high rates. Coalition politics with
Prachanda as PM. Unemployment structure unchanged.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2024.assessment:
Stable structural unemployment at ~10.5%. Nepal's unemployment rate
essentially unchanged across the entire 1991-2024 period except for
COVID spike (2020-2021). Structural unemployment driven by labor
emigration offsetting domestic job creation deficit.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

2025.assessment:
Stable structural unemployment continuing. ILO modeled projection.
Nepal's labor market structure unchanged. Labor emigration safety valve
maintaining apparent stability in unemployment figures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates

1991.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Nepal's democratic transition celebrated
internationally. Full relations with all major powers. Member of UN,
SAARC, and other international organizations. Diplomatic opening
with normalization of Western relations after Panchayat restrictions.

1992.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Aid and diplomatic relations active with
all major powers. SAARC active. UN peacekeeping contributions growing.
Nepal providing troops for UN missions.

1993.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Active SAARC participation. Relations with
India, China, US, UK, Japan all maintained. UN engagement. Nepal's
democratic credential supporting good international standing.

1994.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Democracy functioning. Full bilateral
relations with all major states. No diplomatic disruptions.

1995.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Active UN peacekeeping contributor. SAARC
member. Full relations with India, China, Western countries. No
diplomatic restrictions.

1996.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration despite insurgency launch. International
community supporting Nepal's government against Maoist insurgency.
Security assistance began flowing. No diplomatic disruptions from
insurgency.

1997.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Some human rights concerns from donors.
Full bilateral relations maintained. India-Nepal bilateral treaty
active. UN peacekeeping contributions growing.

1998.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Growing concerns from Western donors about
human rights in counter-insurgency. Full relations maintained with all
partners.

1999.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. New elections reinforced democratic
credential. International support maintained despite insurgency. India-Nepal
cooperation strong.

2000.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration with growing human rights concerns. Western
governments increasingly conditioned security assistance on human rights
improvements. Full diplomatic relations maintained. Nepal still well
integrated.

2001.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Royal massacre shocked international community
but no diplomatic rupture. Post-9/11 Nepal aligned with US counter-terrorism
framework. State of emergency concerned donors. Normal integration.

2002.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration but growing friction. King dismissed
government in October 2002. Western governments expressed concern.
Bilateral relations maintained. Some donor conditions on aid.

2003.assessment:
Full integration with frictions. US designated Maoists as terrorist
organization. Western pressure on human rights. Full bilateral relations
maintained. Nepal diplomatic engagement continuing.

2004.assessment:
Full integration with increasing friction. Some Western bilateral aid
cuts. King dismissing PMs. Diplomatic pressure growing. Relations
maintained overall.

2005.assessment:
Partial diplomatic isolation after royal coup. US, UK, India suspended
military assistance. Some ambassadors recalled for consultations.
Strong international condemnation of royal coup. Nepal approaching
partial isolation with Western governments.

sources.citation: US State Department Nepal; India statement

2006.assessment:
Rapid diplomatic normalization. Jana Andolan II restored democracy.
International aid and diplomatic engagement restored. CPA celebrated
internationally. UNMIN deployment with international support. Full
relations restored within months.

sources.citation: US State Department; UN UNMIN mandate

2007.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Peace process internationally supported.
UNMIN monitoring. India, China, US all engaging with Nepal's transition.
High international interest in Nepal's peace process.

2008.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Republic internationally recognized and
welcomed. US removed Maoists from terrorist list. All major powers
maintaining full relations with new republic. Nepal's diplomatic
standing excellent.

2009.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Post-conflict transition receiving full
international support. Bilateral development partnerships strong.
Nepal growing in international peacekeeping role.

2010.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Constitution deadlock frustrating to donors
but not disrupting diplomatic relations. Nepal's international standing
strong.

2011.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. UNMIN mission ended January 2011 with
conclusion of official peace process monitoring. Nepal fully integrated
in international community. Constitution deadlock noted by donors
without diplomatic consequences.

2012.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. CA dissolution concerning but not disrupting
relations. Nepal's democratic process internationally supported. Full
bilateral relations maintained.

2013.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Second CA elections internationally
observed. Nepal's democratic resilience praised. Full bilateral
relations maintained.

2014.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. All major powers with active embassies.
US, India, China, EU all major partners. Nepal in strong international
standing.

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration with some tension. Post-earthquake international
solidarity overwhelming. India's blockade (September-November 2015)
severely strained India-Nepal relations. Constitution promulgation
celebrated but Madhes protests noted by some governments.

2016.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. India-Nepal relations normalizing after
blockade ended. China engagement deepening (BRI discussions). US
MCC discussions. Nepal diversifying between India and China.

2017.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Federal elections celebrated internationally.
Nepal-China connectivity improving. India-Nepal relations stable.
US engaging on MCC. Nepal balancing between major powers.

2018.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. MCC Compact signed. China-Nepal Railway
discussions. India-Nepal connectivity projects. Nepal active in SAARC,
BIMSTEC, and bilateral partnerships. Full international engagement.

2019.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Some tensions with India over new maps
(Kalapani area). Relations with China strong. US engagement growing.
Nepal active internationally.

2020.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration with some India tensions. Nepal's new map
including Kalapani irritated India. MCC controversy domestically. China
relations warm. US engagement maintaining. Political crisis (parliament
dissolution) noted but not disrupting international relations.

2021.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. India-Nepal relations gradually normalizing.
MCC ratification delayed but relations maintained. China-Nepal active.
Nepal's democratic resilience praised. Full international engagement.

2022.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. MCC Compact ratified February 2022. Elections
internationally observed. Nepal's democratic standing strong. Full bilateral
relations with all major powers.

2023.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Nepal's coalition government navigating
India-China-US relationships. Active peacekeeping contributor (16th
largest). SAARC, BIMSTEC active. Full international standing.

2024.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Oli-NC coalition. Relations with India
normalizing under Oli-Modi understanding. China engagement maintained.
US MCC implementation underway. Nepal active internationally.

2025.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration continuing. Nepal in good international
standing. Active peacekeeping contributor.

1991.assessment:
Negligible FDI. Newly multiparty democratic Nepal had minimal foreign investment.
Economy was heavily agricultural with limited infrastructure for foreign investment.
No significant regulatory framework for attracting FDI.

1996.assessment:
Small but improved FDI in 1996, reaching 0.42% of GDP. Foreign investment
in tourism, hydropower, and manufacturing beginning to develop. Maoist insurgency
launched February 1996 began to create uncertainty. Hydropower attracted Indian
and international investors under early power trade agreements.

1997.assessment:
FDI at 0.47% of GDP, slightly increased. Asian financial crisis affecting
regional investment but Nepal not yet severely impacted as it had limited
financial integration. Maoist insurgency intensifying in rural areas but
urban investment centers not yet significantly affected.

1998.assessment:
FDI declined to 0.25% of GDP. Asian financial crisis regional spillover and
growing Maoist insurgency reduced foreign investor confidence. Tourism-related
FDI declining. Political instability with multiple government changes.

1999.assessment:
FDI dropped further to 0.09% of GDP. Growing insurgency creating uncertainty.
Nepal's investment climate worsening as Maoists extended influence in rural areas.
Limited foreign interest except hydropower and limited manufacturing.

2000.assessment:
FDI turned marginally negative (-0.01% of GDP), indicating net capital outflows.
Maoist insurgency escalating. Investor uncertainty high. Disinvestment beginning
as foreign companies reassessed risk in Nepal's increasingly insecure environment.

2001.assessment:
FDI rebounded to 0.35% of GDP. Some hydropower and tourism investment
continued. Royal massacre June 2001 created brief political uncertainty.
Nepal Army mobilized against Maoists November 2001. Overall FDI volatile
and dominated by single-project investments.

2002.assessment:
FDI negative at -0.10% of GDP. Active insurgency at peak intensity. King
dismissed parliament October 2002. Political crisis combined with military
conflict deterring all but essential foreign investment. Net disinvestment.

2003.assessment:
FDI recovered slightly to 0.23% of GDP. Ceasefire January-August 2003 created
brief window of improved confidence. Hydropower project investments continued.
Post-ceasefire resumption of fighting reduced year-end confidence.

2004.assessment:
FDI marginally negative again (-0.01% GDP). Insurgency resumed at full
intensity post-ceasefire. Royal political interventions added uncertainty.
Nepal's investment climate at severe low. Tourism-related investment
effectively halted.

2005.assessment:
FDI near zero at 0.03% of GDP. Royal coup February 2005 and international
isolation deterred foreign investment. Severe investment climate. Nepal at
political and security low point.

2006.assessment:
FDI slightly negative (-0.07% GDP) despite peace agreement. Jana Andolan II
April 2006 and CPA November 2006 were positive signals but disinvestment
continued through transition uncertainty. Foreign investors cautious despite
political normalization beginning.

2007.assessment:
FDI turned marginally positive again (0.06% GDP). Post-peace process uncertainty
remained. Maoists joining interim government. UNMIN monitoring PLA cantonment.
Investment climate improving slowly but well below potential.

2008.assessment:
FDI near zero (0.01% GDP). Global financial crisis and ongoing political
transition (republic declared May 2008, Maoist government formed) kept
investor confidence minimal. Nepal's investment climate remained weak
despite political milestones.

2009.assessment:
FDI improved to 0.30% of GDP. Post-peace process stabilization attracting
some investment in tourism, hydropower, and telecom. Political deadlock over
constitution still present but security situation much improved.

2010.assessment:
FDI reached 0.55% of GDP, highest since pre-insurgency. Post-peace
investment recovery gaining momentum. Hydropower projects, banking sector,
telecom, and tourism attracting foreign capital. Constitutional Assembly
dysfunction did not halt economic recovery.

2011.assessment:
FDI at 0.44% of GDP. CA expired without constitution May 2011; dissolution
May 2012. Constitutional deadlock creating uncertainty. Investment recovering
but constitutional vacuum dampened confidence.

2012.assessment:
FDI stable at 0.42% of GDP. Constitutional deadlock persisting. Political
uncertainty but improving governance compared to insurgency period. India
remained primary FDI source. Hydropower investment growing.

2013.assessment:
FDI at 0.33% of GDP. New CA elections November 2013 (NC won plurality)
injected optimism. Investment responding to relative security and political
stabilization. Hydro, telecom, manufacturing attracting modest foreign capital.

2014.assessment:
FDI declined to 0.13% of GDP. Slow constitutional progress and labor disputes
in manufacturing zones dampened foreign investment. India-Nepal trade tensions.
Below trend despite overall positive political environment.

2015.assessment:
FDI at 0.21% of GDP despite massive disruptions. Gorkha earthquake April 2015
(8,800+ deaths) and subsequent India blockade September 2015 severely damaged
economy but FDI reflects pre-disaster commitments and reconstruction interest.
New constitution promulgated September 2015.

2016.assessment:
FDI rebounded to 0.43% of GDP. India blockade ended early 2016. Post-earthquake
reconstruction driving some investment. New constitution being implemented.
Federal structure negotiations ongoing but overall more stable.

2017.assessment:
FDI at 0.68% of GDP — highest ever recorded for Nepal. First federal
elections November-December 2017 bringing political stability. Nepal
Investment Summit 2017 attracted foreign interest. Hydropower investment
significant: multiple projects attracting Chinese and Indian investment.
Chinese BRI investment interest growing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS Nepal 2017; Nepal Investment Summit 2017

2018.assessment:
FDI fell sharply to 0.21% of GDP. Investment commitments from 2017 Summit
slow to materialize. Administrative barriers, labor laws, and land acquisition
difficulties limiting actual FDI disbursements. MCC compact signed but
not yet ratified.

2019.assessment:
FDI recovered to 0.54% of GDP. Stable government under Oli. Hydropower,
banking, telecom and tourism investments growing. Nepal Investment Summit 2019
held. Chinese BRI-related project activity. US MCC Compact pending ratification.

2020.assessment:
FDI declined to 0.38% of GDP. COVID-19 disrupted foreign investment globally.
Tourism-related FDI effectively halted. Political crisis: Oli dissolved parliament.
Investment climate weakened by both pandemic and political instability.

2021.assessment:
FDI recovered to 0.53% of GDP despite ongoing political crisis. Supreme Court
restored parliament multiple times. MCC Compact ratified February 2022.
Hydropower and telecom projects continuing. Post-COVID economic reopening.

2022.assessment:
FDI dropped sharply to 0.16% of GDP. Post-COVID current account problems and
forex reserve crisis. Nepal imposed import bans and capital controls that
affected investor confidence. Russia-Ukraine war raised commodity prices
and inflation, reducing investment capacity.

2023.assessment:
FDI marginally improved to 0.18% of GDP. Economic recovery from forex crisis.
Import bans gradually lifted. Investment climate improving but structural
barriers (labor, land, energy costs) continued to limit FDI.

2024.assessment:
FDI at 0.13% of GDP. NC-CPN-UML coalition government relatively stable.
FDI remained well below Nepal's potential given hydropower resources and
strategic location. Structural reform needs (land, labor, energy pricing)
unaddressed limiting foreign investment.

2025.assessment:
Data not yet available for 2025. Based on trajectory, FDI expected to remain
in the minimal range (below 0.5% GDP). Nepal's structural investment barriers
remain without major reform.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI — 2025 data not yet available

1991.assessment:
No foreign military forces stationed in Nepal. Full sovereignty maintained.
Nepal hosted Indian military training mission (India had long-standing bilateral
military cooperation with Nepal). No UN peacekeeping forces in Nepal. Nepal
itself contributed to UN peacekeeping globally.

1992.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Indian military training mission continued under
bilateral agreements. Full Nepal sovereignty maintained. Nepal a significant
UN peacekeeping contributor.

1993.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Indian military training mission present.
Full sovereignty maintained. Nepal-India Peace and Friendship Treaty
of 1950 governs military relationship; Gurkha soldiers serve in Indian/British
armies under long-standing arrangements.

1994.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Limited Indian military advisors. Full sovereignty.
Nepal's military autonomy maintained. Mid-term elections held, political
instability but no foreign military implications.

1995.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Indian military training mission continuing.
Full sovereignty. UML minority government and subsequent Deuba government
did not alter military relationships.

1996.assessment:
Maoist insurgency launched February 1996. No foreign military forces deployed
in response. Nepal Army managed insurgency independently. India and US provided
limited arms/assistance to Nepal Army but no combat forces deployed. Full
sovereign control of military operations.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance South Asia 1996; Nepal Army history

1997.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal Army conducting counterinsurgency operations
independently. US providing limited military assistance (weapons, training).
India providing arms. No foreign combat forces deployed.

1998.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal Army managing insurgency. US and India
providing military equipment assistance. Full sovereignty maintained.
India concerned about Maoist spillover but did not deploy forces.

1999.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal maintaining sovereign control of
counterinsurgency operations. US and India military assistance continued.
No UN peacekeeping forces in Nepal (Nepal was itself a top UN contributor).

2000.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal Army in full control of counterinsurgency.
US providing M16 rifles, other equipment. India providing arms and training.
No foreign military personnel participating in combat operations.

sources.citation: US military assistance to Nepal; IISS South Asia 2000

2001.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal Army mobilized against Maoists November 2001.
Royal massacre June 2001. US and India increased military assistance after
9/11 framing of conflict as counterterrorism. No foreign forces deployed.
Nepal maintained full operational sovereignty.

sources.citation: US IMET program Nepal; IISS South Asia 2001

2002.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal Army conducting major counterinsurgency operations.
US provided $20M+ in military equipment under Emergency Drawdown Authority.
India and UK also providing military assistance. Full sovereign Nepal Army command.

sources.citation: US State Department Nepal military assistance 2002

2003.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. US designated CPN-M as Foreign Terrorist Organization
January 2003. Military assistance continued. Ceasefire January-August 2003.
No foreign military involvement in ceasefire monitoring.

sources.citation: US State Department FTO designation CPN-M 2003; IISS South Asia 2003

2004.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal Army operations resumed after ceasefire collapse.
US and India military assistance continued. Full sovereign military command.
King Gyanendra increasing direct involvement in military matters.

2005.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Royal coup February 2005. US, UK, India suspended
military assistance after coup due to human rights concerns and democratic
backsliding. Despite suspension, no foreign forces deployed. Nepal Army
fully sovereign but aid-deprived.

sources.citation: US suspension of military assistance to Nepal February 2005

2006.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Comprehensive Peace Agreement November 2006.
UNMIN (UN Mission in Nepal) deployment approved December 2006 for
cantonment monitoring — distinct from combat peacekeeping. Full Nepal
sovereignty maintained.

2007.assessment:
UNMIN deployed January 2007. UN Mission in Nepal monitored cantonment of
Maoist PLA forces and Nepal Army. Unarmed civilian mission of ~900 personnel
at peak. Not a combat peacekeeping force. Nepal maintaining full military
sovereignty. US military assistance resumed after democracy restored.

2008.assessment:
UNMIN continued cantonment monitoring. Constituent Assembly elections April 2008.
Republic declared May 2008. Maoist PLA cantonment process ongoing under UNMIN
supervision. Unarmed monitoring mission; Nepal Army fully sovereign.

2009.assessment:
UNMIN continued. Cantonment process stalled over PLA integration into
Nepal Army or rehabilitation into civilian life. UNMIN mandate repeatedly
extended. No combat foreign forces. Full sovereign Nepal Army command.

2010.assessment:
UNMIN continued with reduced mandate. PLA cantonment process prolonged.
Unarmed monitoring mission. Nepal Army fully sovereign. No foreign combat forces.

2011.assessment:
UNMIN mandate ended January 2011 after Nepal government declined renewal.
PLA integration into Nepal Army completed under national framework.
No foreign military presence remaining. Full sovereignty restored completely.

sources.citation: UNMIN closure January 2011; Nepal Army PLA integration

2012.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Full sovereignty. Indian military training
mission continued under bilateral agreements. Nepal Army operating fully
independently. Constitutional Assembly crisis had no military implications.

2013.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Indian military training mission continuing.
US military cooperation resumed. Full sovereignty. New CA elections
November 2013 without any military implications.

2014.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Indian and US military training relationships
continuing. Nepal Army globally active as UN peacekeeping contributor
(one of world's largest). Full sovereignty maintained domestically.

2015.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Gorkha earthquake April 2015 triggered massive
international civilian/humanitarian response but no foreign military forces
stationed in Nepal. Indian and US military helicopters and personnel briefly
present for disaster relief under invited humanitarian assistance — not combat
forces. India blockade September 2015.

sources.citation: Nepal earthquake international response; IISS South Asia 2015

2016.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Post-earthquake reconstruction period. Disaster
relief completed. Indian military training mission continuing. Full sovereignty.
India blockade ended early 2016.

2017.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Federal constitution implemented. First federal
elections November-December 2017. Indian and US military training relationships
continuing. Full sovereignty maintained.

2018.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Nepal balancing India, China, and US military
relationships. China increasing military assistance and exercises with Nepal
Army. India maintained traditional military training relationship.
Full sovereignty. Nepal Army globally active in UN peacekeeping.

2019.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Nepal maintaining military relationships with
India, China, and US simultaneously. Full sovereignty. Nepal among world's
top contributors to UN peacekeeping missions globally.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance South Asia 2019; UN Peacekeeping contributor data

2020.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Political crisis: Oli dissolved parliament twice.
COVID-19 but no foreign military health/enforcement presence. India-Nepal
border tensions over Kalapani/Lipulekh dispute but no military confrontation.
Full sovereignty maintained.

2021.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Political crisis resolved by Supreme Court.
India-Nepal border tension continued but no military escalation.
Full sovereignty maintained. Nepal Army operating independently.

2022.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Stable post-election period. Nepal maintaining
military relationships with India, China, and US. Full sovereignty.
Nepal among top 5 UN peacekeeping contributors globally.

2023.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Coalition government continued. Nepal maintaining
balanced military-diplomatic relationships with all major powers. Full sovereignty.

2024.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. NC-CPN-UML coalition government. Nepal continuing
military training relationships with India, China, and US. Full sovereignty.
Nepal remained among world's largest UN peacekeeping contributors.

2025.assessment:
No foreign combat forces. Continuing balanced military relationships.
Full sovereignty maintained. Nepal Army active in UN peacekeeping globally.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance South Asia ongoing

1991.assessment:
Nepal hosting significant Bhutanese refugee inflows beginning 1991-1993.
Approximately 100,000 ethnic Nepali (Lhotshampa) expelled from Bhutan
settled in UNHCR camps in eastern Nepal (Jhapa and Morang districts).
No significant outflow of Nepali refugees at this point. Nepal population
approximately 19 million. Bhutanese refugees approximately 0.5% of population.

sources.citation: UNHCR Bhutanese Refugee Situation; Human Rights Watch Bhutan 1993

1992.assessment:
Bhutanese refugee influx peaked. Approximately 86,000-100,000 Lhotshampa
refugees in Nepal camps by end 1992. No outflow of Nepali nationals as
refugees. Nepal managing large refugee hosting burden with limited resources.

1993.assessment:
Bhutanese refugee camps established and stabilized. Nepal hosting approximately
100,000 Bhutanese refugees. Nepal-Bhutan bilateral negotiations for refugee
return initiated (and would drag on for over a decade). No significant
outflow of Nepali nationals.

sources.citation: UNHCR Bhutanese refugee situation; Nepal-Bhutan joint committee

1994.assessment:
Bhutanese refugees stabilized in camps. Nepal hosting approximately 100,000.
Nepal-Bhutan talks ongoing but no repatriation progress. No Nepali refugee
outflows. Maoist insurgency not yet launched; internal security stable.

1995.assessment:
Continuing situation: Nepal hosting ~100,000 Bhutanese refugees.
Nepal-Bhutan repatriation talks stalled. No Nepali refugee outflow.
Political instability in Nepal (UML minority government, Deuba government)
but no forced displacement of Nepali nationals.

1996.assessment:
Maoist insurgency launched February 1996. Early insurgency did not yet produce
significant IDPs or refugee outflows. Nepal hosting ~100,000 Bhutanese.
Insurgency initially low-intensity in remote hill districts.

sources.citation: UNHCR South Asia 1996; INSEC Nepal annual report 1996

1997.assessment:
Growing internal displacement from insurgency areas. Rural population
moving to district headquarters and Kathmandu Valley as Maoists expanded
control. IDPs (internally displaced) growing but most displacement internal.
Nepal-Bhutan negotiations continued without progress.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal Internal Displacement; UNHCR South Asia 1997

1998.assessment:
Increasing internal displacement from Maoist insurgency areas. Tens of thousands
displaced internally but not crossing borders as refugees. Nepal hosting ~100,000
Bhutanese. No significant outflow of Nepali nationals to other countries.

1999.assessment:
Internal displacement from insurgency significant but not reaching refugee
level cross-border flows. Nepal-Bhutan talks: Bhutanese refugees registered,
verification process ongoing. Bhutan refusing repatriation of most refugees.

sources.citation: UNHCR South Asia 1999; Nepal-Bhutan verification process

2000.assessment:
Internal displacement from insurgency growing significantly. Estimated
50,000-100,000 IDPs within Nepal by 2000-2001. No major cross-border refugee
outflow. Bhutanese refugees in Nepal still ~100,000. Nepal's population ~23 million.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal displacement estimates 2000-2001; UNHCR South Asia

2001.assessment:
Nepal Army mobilized November 2001. Internal displacement sharply accelerating.
Royal massacre June 2001. IDMC estimated 100,000-200,000 IDPs by end of 2001.
Some Nepali nationals seeking asylum in India informally but no formal cross-border
refugee recognition. Nepal hosting Bhutanese still.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal IDP data 2001; INSEC Nepal human rights yearbook 2001

2002.assessment:
Internal displacement at peak levels. IDMC estimated 200,000-400,000 IDPs at
peak of conflict 2002-2005. Rural population fleeing to urban centers to escape
Maoist forced recruitment and army operations. Not cross-border refugee flows
but massive internal displacement. Nepal hosting Bhutanese continued.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal IDP estimates 2002; Human Rights Watch Nepal 2002

2003.assessment:
Continuing internal displacement. Ceasefire January-August 2003 provided some
relief and early returns. Post-ceasefire resumption of fighting renewed
displacement. IDPs at 200,000-300,000 range. Nepal-Bhutan pilot repatriation
of Bhutanese refugees failed to materialize meaningfully.

2004.assessment:
High internal displacement. Approximately 200,000-300,000 IDPs.
Royal political interventions adding uncertainty. Bhutanese refugees:
third-country resettlement to US and Europe beginning as bilateral talks
with Bhutan collapsed.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal 2004; UNHCR Bhutanese third-country resettlement program

2005.assessment:
Royal coup February 2005 and continued insurgency. Internal displacement
remained high. Approximately 200,000-300,000 IDPs within Nepal. Some Nepali
civil society activists and political party members sought asylum in India
during royal repression. Third-country resettlement of Bhutanese accelerating.

2006.assessment:
Peace agreement November 2006. IDPs beginning to return. Estimated 150,000-200,000
IDPs at year start, declining as security improved. Bhutanese third-country
resettlement to US ongoing: approximately 60,000 Bhutanese would eventually
resettle in US 2007-2015.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal 2006; UNHCR Bhutanese resettlement program

2007.assessment:
Significant IDP returns following peace agreement. UNMIN monitoring cantonment.
Net population movement from displacement to return. Bhutanese refugees:
large-scale third-country resettlement to US begun; over 6,000 departed 2007.
Nepal continuing to host remaining Bhutanese in camps.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal 2007; UNHCR Nepal country operation 2007

2008.assessment:
IDP returns largely complete. Republic declared May 2008. Bhutanese
third-country resettlement continuing at scale: ~16,000 departed 2008.
Nepal's internal displacement crisis resolving. Continuing to host remaining
Bhutanese in camps (~107,000 peak, declining with resettlement).

sources.citation: UNHCR Nepal 2008 annual report; IDMC Nepal

2009.assessment:
Minimal internal displacement. Bhutanese resettlement accelerating:
~18,000 departed 2009. Nepal hosted approximately 88,000 remaining Bhutanese
(down from ~107,000 at peak). Tarai/Madhes tensions created minor temporary
displacement but not major refugee flows.

2010.assessment:
Very low internal displacement. Bhutanese third-country resettlement
ongoing (~12,000 departed 2010; approximately 70,000 remaining in camps).
Nepal's conflict-related displacement resolved. Country stable.
Nepal itself contributing troops to UN peacekeeping globally.

2013.assessment:
Minimal displacement. New CA elected November 2013. Bhutanese third-country
resettlement ~10,000/year; approximately 30,000 remaining in Nepal camps.
Country stable. No new displacement drivers.

2014.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Nepal stable. Bhutanese resettlement: approximately
20,000 remaining. Constitution drafting nearing completion. No significant
displacement events.

2015.assessment:
Gorkha earthquake April 2015 (7.8 magnitude; 8,800+ deaths) caused massive
temporary internal displacement — approximately 2.8 million people displaced
temporarily from damaged/destroyed homes. However this was natural disaster
displacement, largely resolved by 2016. No refugee outflows from Nepal.
India blockade September 2015 caused hardship but not displacement.
Bhutanese remaining ~13,000 in camps.

sources.citation: IDMC Nepal Earthquake 2015; OCHA Nepal earthquake response

2016.assessment:
Post-earthquake displacement largely resolved. Returns mostly complete.
India blockade ended early 2016. Bhutanese remaining approximately 7,000-10,000
in Nepal camps (most resettled; remaining declining to make decision).
Minimal new displacement. Country stable.

2017.assessment:
Stable minimal displacement. Federal elections November-December 2017
passed without displacement events. Bhutanese remaining in Nepal camps:
approximately 6,000-7,000 (those who declined resettlement or return).
Nepal hosting Tibetan refugees (~12,000-15,000 in Nepal) under China pressure
to limit their activities.

sources.citation: UNHCR Nepal 2017; HRW Tibetan refugees Nepal

2018.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. Bhutanese resettlement essentially complete
(~5,000 remaining, refusing resettlement options). Nepal hosting ~12,000
Tibetan refugees under UNHCR mandate. China pressure limits Tibetan
political activity in Nepal. Stable security and political environment.

2019.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. Nepal hosting approximately 12,000 Tibetan
refugees and ~5,000 remaining Bhutanese. Some influx of other refugees
and asylum seekers (Rohingya, others). No major displacement events.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 caused return of migrant workers from India (estimated 300,000+
returned to Nepal across open border). Not formally refugee flows but
significant migration disruption. Political crisis (Oli parliament
dissolution) but no domestic displacement. Hosting Tibetan and remaining
Bhutanese refugees as before.

sources.citation: IOM Nepal COVID-19 migrant worker returns; UNHCR Nepal 2020

2021.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Political crisis resolved. Myanmar coup February 2021
created some small-scale Burmese asylum seeker arrivals in Nepal
(very limited). Nepal hosting Tibetan and remaining Bhutanese refugees.
Stable overall situation.

2022.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Parliamentary elections November 2022 stable.
Nepal continuing to host Tibetan refugees (~12,000) and remaining Bhutanese
(~5,000). Small number of other asylum seekers. No significant displacement
drivers. Economic crisis (forex reserves) did not cause displacement.

2023.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. Nepal hosting Tibetan and Bhutanese refugees.
No new displacement events. Country politically stable under coalition
government.

2024.assessment:
Minimal displacement. NC-CPN-UML coalition government. Nepal hosting
approximately 12,000 Tibetan refugees and ~4,000-5,000 remaining Bhutanese.
No new significant displacement. Stable political and security environment.

2025.assessment:
Continuing stable minimal displacement. Nepal hosting Tibetan and Bhutanese
refugees. No new displacement drivers evident. Status as of early 2026.

1991.assessment:
No international sanctions against Nepal. Democratic transition in 1990
was welcomed internationally. Aid and development assistance flowing.
Nepal in good standing with international community.

1993.assessment:
No sanctions. Nepal's democratic government receiving full international
support. Foreign aid major component of development budget. No
sanctions or diplomatic restrictions.

1994.assessment:
No sanctions. Aid flowing. International community supportive of
Nepal's democratic development. Multiple coalition governments but
no international concerns requiring sanctions.

1995.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. Aid flowing. Nepal
pre-insurgency with full international support.

1996.assessment:
No sanctions. Maoist insurgency launched but not yet triggering
international sanction concerns. Nepal government receiving security
assistance from India and some bilateral donors for counter-insurgency.

1997.assessment:
No sanctions. International community concerned about insurgency but
not imposing restrictions on Nepal government. Aid and development
assistance maintained.

1998.assessment:
No sanctions. Some Western concern about human rights in counter-insurgency
but not resulting in sanctions. US providing some security assistance.
India-Nepal security cooperation ongoing.

1999.assessment:
No sanctions. Nepal receiving Western security assistance (helicopters,
training). India providing significant security cooperation. International
community supporting Nepal's democratic government against Maoist
insurgency.

2000.assessment:
No sanctions but growing human rights concerns. Human Rights Watch
documenting security force abuses in counter-insurgency. Some US/UK
pressure on human rights without formal sanctions. Aid continued.

sources.citation: US State Department Human Rights Report Nepal

2001.assessment:
No sanctions. After 9/11, Nepal's counter-insurgency received US
post-9/11 counter-terrorism support framing. Royal massacre was domestic
tragedy but did not trigger sanctions. Security assistance expanded.

2002.assessment:
No sanctions but growing concerns. King dismissed government October
2002. Western donors expressing concern but not imposing sanctions.
Aid maintained. US security assistance: $20 million in military equipment.

2003.assessment:
No formal sanctions but increasing international pressure on human rights.
US designating CPN-Maoist as terrorist organization. Some donor
governments conditioning aid on human rights improvements. No formal
sanctions regime.

2004.assessment:
No formal sanctions. King dismissing PMs. Growing donor concern.
Some bilateral aid cuts by UK and US over human rights. India-Nepal
security cooperation continued.

sources.citation: UK DFID, US State Department Nepal aid

2005.assessment:
Near-sanctions response to royal coup. US, UK, India suspended military
assistance after February 2005 royal coup. Not formal sanctions but
suspension of security aid. India recalled ambassador briefly. Strong
international condemnation. Not full sanctions regime.

sources.citation: US Congress Nepal Democracy Act 2005; UK India statements

2006.assessment:
Military aid restrictions lifted after Jana Andolan II restored democracy.
Full international aid and engagement resumed. No sanctions in effect.
International community celebrating Nepal's democratic restoration.

2007.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. UNMIN deployed with
international support. Peace process internationally supported. Aid
flows resumed.

2008.assessment:
No sanctions. Republic internationally recognized. US State Department
removed Maoists from terrorist designation. Full international engagement
with Nepal's new federal republic.

2009.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. Post-conflict transition.
International community supporting Nepal's peace process through UNMIN
and bilateral assistance.

2010.assessment:
No sanctions. International community engaged. Political deadlock on
constitution concerning to donors but not triggering sanctions. Aid
maintained. UNMIN monitoring peace process.

2011.assessment:
No sanctions. UNMIN mission ended January 2011. International community
continuing to support Nepal's peace process. Bilateral aid maintained.
No concerns requiring sanctions.

2012.assessment:
No sanctions. International support for Nepal's democratic process
continuing. CA dissolution concerning to donors but not triggering
sanctions. Aid maintained to support democratic transition.

2013.assessment:
No sanctions. International observers supporting second CA elections.
Aid and engagement full. International community engaged in Nepal's
democratic consolidation.

2014.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. Constitution drafting.
Aid and development assistance strong. US, UK, Japan, India all major
development partners.

2015.assessment:
No sanctions. Post-earthquake international assistance flooding in.
India blockade criticized internationally but India not sanctioned.
Madhes protests and their suppression noted but no sanctions on Nepal.
Constitution promulgation internationally noted without sanctions response.

2016.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. Federal implementation
internationally supported. Aid flows maintained. Nepal in good standing
with international community.

2017.assessment:
No sanctions. Federal elections internationally observed and praised.
