Nepal in good standing. MCC Compact discussions beginning. US, India,
China all engaged with Nepal.

2018.assessment:
No sanctions. MCC Compact signed (US Millennium Challenge Corporation
$500 million). Full international engagement. China BRI discussions.
India-Nepal development cooperation. No sanctions concerns.

2019.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. MCC Compact awaiting
parliament ratification. Nepal's democracy in good standing.
No sanctions concerns.

2020.assessment:
No sanctions. International concern about Oli's dissolution of parliament
but no sanctions response. MCC Compact controversy but no sanctions.
Full international engagement.

2021.assessment:
No sanctions. Democracy restored through Supreme Court. Deuba PM.
MCC Compact eventually ratified by parliament in February 2022.
Full international engagement. Nepal democracy praised for resilience.

2022.assessment:
No sanctions. MCC Compact ratified February 2022 — major US-Nepal
milestone. Elections held. Full international engagement. Nepal in
good international standing.

2023.assessment:
No sanctions. Nepal in good international standing. Coalition politics
but full international engagement. Aid and development partnerships
maintained with US, India, China, Europe.

2024.assessment:
No sanctions. NC-CPN-UML coalition under Oli. Full international
engagement. Nepal good standing with international community.
No sanctions or restrictions in place.

2025.assessment:
No sanctions. Nepal in good international standing. Full engagement
continuing.

1991.assessment:
Nepal FH CL=4 in 1991 — Partly Free. New democracy with significant
civil liberties improvements from Panchayat era. Press freedom growing.
Political parties operating. Some restrictions on assembly and press
remaining from transition period.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 1991

1992.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Civil liberties improving. Free press developing.
Assembly rights. Political party freedom. Courts functioning. Nepal
making civil liberties progress as new democracy.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World

1993.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Civil liberties broadly maintained. Press freedom
active. Judiciary functioning. Political freedoms generally respected.
Some concerns about police conduct.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World

1994.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Civil liberties maintaining. Elections held. Press
free. Political parties operating. Some police abuse reported. Overall
a partially free environment.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World

1995.assessment:
CL=4. Civil liberties broadly maintained. Pre-insurgency period. Free
press. Political parties operating. Some concerns about corruption and
police conduct. Mostly free environment.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World

1996.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Insurgency launched February 1996 beginning to
restrict civil liberties in western districts. Emergency measures in
affected areas. Press coverage of conflict restricted. Overall still
partly free nationally.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World

1997.assessment:
CL=4. Insurgency growing. Security forces conducting operations with
reports of human rights violations. Civil liberties restricting in
conflict districts. Nationally still partly free but deteriorating in
west.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World

1998.assessment:
CL=4. Continuing deterioration from conflict. Police force conducting
brutal counter-insurgency operations (documented by Amnesty International).
Extrajudicial killings in conflict zones. Press freedom restricted in
affected areas. Nationally partly free.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World; Amnesty International Nepal

1999.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Elections held nationally. Civil liberties maintained
in urban areas. Growing human rights violations in conflict zones.
Journalists covering insurgency harassed by both sides.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World

2000.assessment:
FH downgraded CL to 5 as insurgency intensified. Security forces
conducting major operations with documented extrajudicial killings.
Torture of detainees. Maoists also committing abuses. Significant
restrictions on civil liberties in conflict zones.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2000

2001.assessment:
CL=5. Significant restrictions. Royal massacre traumatized society. State
of emergency from November 2001. Military operations with serious human
rights violations. Press heavily restricted in conflict zones. Significant
restrictions on civil liberties nationally.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2001

2002.assessment:
CL=5. Significant restrictions. Parliament dismissed. Emergency law.
Press freedom severely curtailed. NHRC documenting widespread
extrajudicial killings. Both sides committing serious abuses.
Civil liberties severely degraded.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2002

2003.assessment:
CL=5. Ceasefire briefly improved civil liberties situation. But resumed
conflict (August 2003) brought restrictions back. No elected parliament.
Human rights abuses by both sides continuing. Significant restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2003

2004.assessment:
CL=5. Continued significant restrictions. King dismissing governments.
No elected parliament. Conflict-related abuses continuing. Press freedom
restricted. Human rights defenders targeted. Significant civil liberties
restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2004

2005.assessment:
FH downgraded to CL=6 (Not Free) after royal coup February 2005. King
Gyanendra suspended civil liberties, arrested political leaders, shut down
press, banned political parties. Mobile phones, internet restricted.
No free media. Most severe civil liberties restrictions since monarchy.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005

2006.assessment:
FH upgraded back to CL=4 after April 2006 Jana Andolan II restored
democracy. Civil liberties rapidly restored with end of royal rule
and peace agreement. Press freedom restored. Political party activity
resumed. Major improvement within the year.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2006

2007.assessment:
CL=4. Civil liberties improving. Post-peace transition. Press freedom
restored. Political parties operating. Some concerns about YCL (Maoist
youth) extortion. Generally improving civil liberties trajectory.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2007

2008.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Republic declared. Civil liberties broadly maintained.
Press freedom. Political freedoms. Some concerns about political interference
in judiciary. Generally improving civil liberties environment.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2008

2009.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Civil liberties broadly maintained despite political
instability. Press freedom. Political freedoms. Some concerns about
impunity for conflict-era abuses.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2009

2010.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Civil liberties maintained. Press freedom, assembly
rights, association rights broadly respected. Political freedoms active.
Constitution deadlock frustrating but not impacting civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2010

2011.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Civil liberties maintained despite constitutional
crisis. Free media. Political freedoms. CA expired without constitution
but civil liberties unaffected.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2011

2012.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. CA dissolved May 2012. Political vacuum but civil
liberties maintained. Press freedom. Political freedoms. Democratic
culture preserved through institutional crisis.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2012

2013.assessment:
CL=4. Second CA elections November 2013. Vibrant electoral politics.
Press free. Civil liberties broadly maintained throughout the political
transition period.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2013

2014.assessment:
CL=4. Partly Free. Civil liberties broadly maintained. Constitution
drafting ongoing. Free media environment. Political freedoms active.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2014

2015.assessment:
CL=4. Constitution promulgated September 2015. Madhes protests from
September resulting in security forces killing 50+ protesters. India
blockade. Press coverage of protests restricted. Civil liberties strained
during constitution protests but overall partly free.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2015

2016.assessment:
CL=4. Civil liberties recovering after 2015 tensions. India blockade
ended. Madhes protests reduced. Press freedom maintained. Federal
implementation giving more local civil liberties space.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2016

2017.assessment:
FH improved CL to 3 (Free) as federal elections proceeded. Civil
liberties improving with democratic consolidation. Federal system
providing greater local autonomy. Free press. Strong civic society.
Nepal entering Free category for first time.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2017

2018.assessment:
CL=3. Free. Civil liberties broadly maintained. Federal democratic
system functional. Press freedom. Freedom of assembly. Political
freedoms respected. Nepal's best civil liberties performance.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2018

2019.assessment:
CL=3. Free. Civil liberties maintained. Some concerns about Electronic
Transactions Act used against journalists. Overall free environment.
Nepal performing well on civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2019

2020.assessment:
CL=3. Free. Civil liberties maintained despite COVID-19 restrictions
and political crisis. Some media restrictions. Parliament dissolution
contested. Judiciary assertive protecting civil liberties. Nepal
remaining free.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2020

2021.assessment:
CL=3. Free. Civil liberties maintained. Political crisis resolved through
courts. Press freedom active. Political freedoms. Some concerns about
social media regulation attempts. Nepal broadly free.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2021

2022.assessment:
CL=3. Free. Civil liberties broadly maintained. Elections held freely.
Press freedom. Political freedoms. Nepal maintaining Free status in
South Asian context where most neighbors are Partly Free or less.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2022

2023.assessment:
CL=3. Free. Civil liberties maintained. Coalition politics but press
freedom and political freedoms maintained. Nepal's civil liberties
performance among best in South Asia.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2023

2024.assessment:
CL=3. Free. Civil liberties broadly maintained. Oli-NC coalition
government. Press freedom. Political freedoms. Nepal stable in Free
category for civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2024

2025.assessment:
Civil liberties broadly maintained continuing. Nepal remaining in Free
category for civil liberties.

1991.assessment:
Post-1990 democratic transition. Elite fragmentation: NC, CPN-UML,
and smaller parties competing. New democratic elite emerging from
Panchayat opposition. King Birendra accepted constitutional role.
Moderate fragmentation in new multiparty system.

1992.assessment:
Moderate elite fragmentation. NC dominant. CPN-UML opposition. Multiple
parties competing. Constitutional monarchy. Consensus on democratic
framework but strong party competition.

1993.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation. Coalition politics dominant — no single party
with majority. Multiple government changes. Cross-party cooperation
needed. Elite consensus on democratic system but sharp policy disagreements.

1994.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation. NC government lost confidence vote, called
midterm elections. CPN-UML won minority government. No majority
possible without coalition. Elite competition intense.

1995.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. No stable majority possible. Multiple PM
changes. Corruption scandals eroding confidence in democratic elites.
CPN-M's Prachanda preparing for armed insurgency as legal politics seen
as captured by established elites.

1996.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. Insurgency launched. Democratic parties unable
to agree on response. Multiple PM changes. Maoists outside democratic
elite entirely. Three-way split: parliamentary parties, king, Maoists.

1997.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. Insurgency expanding. Parliamentary parties
in disarray. King's role expanding. Maoists outside system. No
coherent elite response to insurgency.

1998.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. Multiple PM changes. Parties unable to form
stable government. Insurgency growing. Elite fragmentation enabling
Maoist expansion.

1999.assessment:
Moderate-significant fragmentation. 1999 elections gave NC majority.
Some elite consolidation around NC government. But insurgency response
divided. Girija Prasad Koirala PM — some stability.

2000.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. Insurgency at major intensity. Multiple PM
changes within year. Elite unable to present unified response. King's
role growing. Three-way fragmentation deepening.

2001.assessment:
Acute fragmentation. Royal massacre June 2001 traumatized political elite.
King Gyanendra as new king viewed suspiciously by parties. Military
deployed. Ceasefire-dialogue-collapse cycle. Maoist elite autonomous
from democratic elites. Deeply fragmented.

2002.assessment:
Active power struggle. King Gyanendra dismissing government October 2002.
Parliamentary parties in opposition. Military reporting to king. Maoists
fighting. Three active competing elite factions — democratic parties,
royalists, Maoists.

2003.assessment:
Active power struggle continuing. King appointing governments. Parties
in opposition. Maoists fighting. Failed ceasefire showed fragmentation.
Peace talks collapsed due to three-way elite conflict.

2004.assessment:
Active power struggle. King dismissing and appointing governments. Deuba
arrested. Parties in coalition against king. Maoists fighting. Three-way
conflict among king, parties, and Maoists.

2005.assessment:
Active power struggle but historic realignment. Royal coup February 2005.
Twelve-Point Understanding November 2005 — parties and Maoists agreed
against king. King isolated. Two factions (parties + Maoists) vs
royalists. Seeds of peace planted.

2006.assessment:
Elite convergence happening. Jana Andolan II successful; parties and
Maoists allied against king. King marginalized. Comprehensive Peace
Agreement brought Maoists into democratic fold. Elite cohesion significantly
improved with major adversaries now collaborating.

2007.assessment:
Improving elite cohesion under peace process. Maoists, NC, CPN-UML,
and smaller parties in interim government together. King sidelined.
Multi-party elite collaboration on CA elections. Some internal tensions
but broadly cohesive transitional framework.

2008.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation. CA election results created new tensions —
Maoists won largest share but not majority. Republic declared with
broad consensus. Prachanda as first PM — elite sharing power.
Multiple parties in coalition.

2009.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. Prachanda resigned May 2009 in dispute
over army chief dismissal. Major elite conflict between Maoists and
army/president. Coalition fragmentation high. Multiple PM attempts
failed before Madhav Nepal became PM.

2010.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. Constitution deadlock due to elite disagreement
on federalism, religion, form of government. Multiple PM attempts.
NC-Maoist power struggle dominant. CPN-UML pivoting between parties.
Elite unable to agree on constitutional framework.

2011.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. CA expired without constitution. Deadlock
between NC, CPN-UML, and Maoists on constitutional issues. No PM with
stable majority. Elite paralysis on core political questions.

2012.assessment:
Continued significant fragmentation. CA dissolved May 2012 without
constitution due to elite deadlock. Technocratic government formed to
manage elections. Elite unable to agree on basic constitutional structure.

2013.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation. Second CA elections produced NC majority. Some
elite consolidation around NC government. Maoists experienced by defeat.
Constitution writing restarted with better prospects.

2014.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation. NC-CPN-UML agreement on constitution framework
building. Maoist parties adjusting positions. Some elite convergence
on key issues needed for constitution. Progress being made.

2015.assessment:
Partial elite cohesion. NC-CPN-UML-UCPN(M) agreement on constitution
reached September 2015 (passed despite Madhes opposition). Constitution
promulgated — major elite achievement. But Madhes and ethnic parties
excluded, creating new fragmentation. Partial.

2016.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation. Negotiations with Madhes parties ongoing.
Multiple PM changes. NC-Maoist-UML coalition dynamics shifting. Elite
managing Madhes integration into constitutional framework.

2017.assessment:
Moderate cohesion. Left alliance (Maoists + CPN-UML) formed for elections.
NC in opposition. CPN-UML + Maoist alliance won majority. Left alliance
showing unusual elite coordination. Federal elections conducted successfully
with broad elite acceptance.

2018.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation after NCP formation. NCP (merger of CPN-UML
and UCPN-M) under Oli-Prachanda leadership. Large majority. But
internal NCP tensions between Oli and Prachanda factions. NC in
strong opposition. Overall manageable fragmentation.

2019.assessment:
Moderate-significant fragmentation. NCP internal conflict between Oli
and Prachanda growing. NC in opposition. Oli assertive PM; Prachanda
increasingly sidelined. NCP unity under strain. Fragmentation growing
within ruling party.

2020.assessment:
Active power struggle. Oli vs Prachanda conflict became open. Oli
dissolved parliament twice. NCP split into two factions. Supreme
Court interventions multiple times. Elite conflict at height —
active power struggle within ruling party and across parties.

2021.assessment:
Active power struggle resolved through institutions. Supreme Court
restored parliament. Deuba became PM with Maoist support. NCP formally
split into NC-allied Maoists and Oli's CPN-UML. New coalition forming.
Fragmentation significant but managed through institutions.

2022.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. November 2022 elections fragmented: no
party with majority. NC, Maoists, CPN-UML all significant. Multiple
coalition talks. Prachanda formed government with changing coalitions.
Complex multiparty elite competition.

2023.assessment:
Significant fragmentation. Multiple coalition changes. Prachanda
switching alliance partners (first with NC, then with CPN-UML, then
back). Complex multi-party elite competition. No stable majority.
Elite fragmentation high but managed through coalition politics.

2024.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation. NC-CPN-UML coalition formed July 2024 with
Oli as PM — unusual as these were traditional rivals. Coalition
commanded significant parliamentary majority. Maoists in opposition.
Moderate fragmentation with unusual large-party coalition.

2025.assessment:
Moderate fragmentation continuing. NC-CPN-UML coalition governing.
Nepal's elite competition managed through democratic institutions.

1991.assessment:
Multiparty democracy established in 1990. First multiparty elections held
May 1991. Parliament functioning. Constitutional monarchy. NC won majority.
Institutions newly established but functioning. Partially functional
with democratic optimism.

1992.assessment:
Institutions functioning. Parliament active. Multiple parties represented.
Judiciary beginning independence. Civil service reform. Political
instability with coalition pressures but institutions working.

1993.assessment:
Institutions functioning with some instability. Coalition politics.
Parliament active but unstable. Judiciary functioning. Electoral
commission independent. Some patronage concerns.

1994.assessment:
Midterm elections held after NC vote of no confidence against itself.
CPN-UML won minority government. Electoral institutions functional.
Parliament functioning. Democratic competition working.

1995.assessment:
Institutions functioning but coalition instability creating governance
problems. Multiple PM changes. Corruption scandals. CPN-M preparing
insurgency as formal legal channels seen as failing rural poor.
Institutional quality limited.

1996.assessment:
Institutions functioning but increasingly challenged by insurgency.
Parliament active. Government functioning in urban areas. Security
forces being deployed. Institutional reach declining in western districts.
Partially functional with insurgency beginning.

1997.assessment:
Institutions declining in effectiveness. Insurgency challenging government
authority in growing areas. Security forces responding. Parliament
continuing but instability high. Institutional reach declining.

1998.assessment:
Institutional effectiveness declining. Government unable to extend
services in Maoist-controlled areas. Parliament continuing but conflict
dominating agenda. Elections becoming difficult in affected districts.

1999.assessment:
Continued decline in institutional effectiveness in conflict areas.
National elections held in 1999 but insecurity in western Nepal.
Parliament functioning. Judiciary active. Security situation eroding
institutional reach in many districts.

2000.assessment:
Severely degraded institutions in conflict areas. Government institutions
absent from much of rural western and mid-hill Nepal. Urban institutions
functioning. National parliament active. Dual authority — government and
Maoist People's Government in much of countryside.

2001.assessment:
Institutional crisis. Royal massacre June 2001. State of emergency
November 2001. Army deployed. Parliament functioning but traumatized.
King Gyanendra taking throne. Emergency governance replacing normal
institutions. Severely degraded overall.

2002.assessment:
Institutional collapse in much of country. King Gyanendra dismissed
government in October 2002. Emergency rule. Army controlling security.
Parliament dismissed. No functioning government in most of rural Nepal.
Institutional framework highly degraded.

2003.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded. King appointing governments. No elected
parliament. Military operations ongoing. Maoist government in rural areas.
International pressure for restoring democracy.

2004.assessment:
Continued institutional degradation. King appointing and dismissing
governments. Sher Bahadur Deuba dismissed and arrested. No elected
parliament. Rule by decree. Institutional legitimacy severely compromised.

2005.assessment:
Royal coup February 2005. King Gyanendra took direct power. All democratic
institutions suspended. Parliament dismissed. Emergency rule. No
independent press. Political parties banned. Institutional collapse
at national level. Post-coup governance.

2006.assessment:
Democratic institutions restored through Jana Andolan II (April 2006).
Parliament reinstated. CPA November 2006. UNMIN deployed. Interim
government formed. Rapid institutional recovery underway. From
collapsed to partially functional within year.

2007.assessment:
Institutions recovering rapidly. Interim constitution adopted. Maoists
entered government. CA elections prepared. UNMIN monitoring. Institutions
broadly functioning in transition mode. Partially functional with
significant recovery.

2008.assessment:
CA elected April 2008 — major institutional milestone. Republic declared
May 2008. New institutions being built. Parliament (CA) functional.
Government operating. Judiciary active. Institutions broadly functional
for new republic.

2009.assessment:
Institutions functioning in transition. Prachanda resigned (first PM
of republic). Multiple PM changes. CA working on constitution.
Institutional instability but core functioning maintained.

2010.assessment:
Institutions broadly functioning despite political deadlock. CA active
(but unable to agree on constitution). Multiple PM changes. Judiciary
functioning. Elections not held. Institutional dysfunction at political
level but state services maintained.

2011.assessment:
CA expired without constitution (May 2011). Major institutional crisis.
Caretaker government. No constitution. Supreme Court repeatedly
extending CA's term. Institutional impasse. Partially functional
but constitutional crisis major.

2012.assessment:
CA dissolved May 2012 without constitution. Political vacuum. No elected
parliament. Caretaker government. Presidential system preventing total
collapse. Institutional impasse at peak. New elections required.

2013.assessment:
Second CA elected November 2013. Institutional recovery. Technocratic
caretaker government managed transition successfully. CA functioning.
Judiciary active. Nepal's democratic resilience shown.

2014.assessment:
Institutions functioning. Constitution drafting making progress under
NC-CPN-UML agreement. Parliament active. Judiciary functioning.
Government providing services. Nepal moving toward institutional stability.

2015.assessment:
Earthquake April 2015 tested institutions — emergency response weak
but government functioning. Constitution promulgated September 2015
— major milestone. However, India blockade (September-November 2015)
and Madhes protests created new tensions. Partially functional overall.

2016.assessment:
Federal constitution being implemented. New institutions at provincial
and local levels being planned. India blockade ended. PM changes
continuing. Parliamentary democracy functioning. Partially functional
in transition to federalism.

2017.assessment:
Major institutional milestone — first federal elections November-December
2017. Provincial and local elections held. Federal structure becoming
operative. Institutions at all three levels (federal, provincial, local)
established. Significant institutional progress.

2018.assessment:
Federal institutions increasingly operational. NCP majority government
stable. Provincial and local governments functioning. Nepal's new
federal system bedding in. Institutional quality improving with stability.

2019.assessment:
Federal institutions consolidating. Parliament functioning. NCP government
stable (temporarily). Rule of law improving. Constitutional court
functioning. Institutions most functional since 1990.

2020.assessment:
Constitutional crisis — Oli dissolved parliament December 2020 (ruled
unconstitutional March 2021). Supreme Court assertive. Parliament
repeatedly dissolved and restored. Institutions resilient through crisis.
COVID-19 emergency. Partially functional despite constitutional crisis.

2021.assessment:
Parliament restored by Supreme Court. Deuba became PM. Political
instability but institutional resilience demonstrated. NCP formally
split. Multiple party realignments. Institutions functional through
turbulence. Democracy maintained.

2022.assessment:
November 2022 elections successfully held — major institutional
demonstration. Prachanda coalition formed. Electoral institutions
functioning. Coalition government management challenging but democratic.
Partially functional democracy.

2023.assessment:
Institutions functioning under coalition government. Prachanda PM.
Multiple coalition changes. Parliament functioning. Judiciary active.
Nepal's institutions managing complex coalition politics.

2024.assessment:
Institutions functioning. Oli returned as PM July 2024 in NC-CPN-UML
coalition. Parliament active. Judiciary independent. Nepal's federal
democratic institutions broadly functional. Political instability
managed through institutional processes.

2025.assessment:
Institutions partially functional continuing. Nepal's democratic system
broadly stable. No signs of major institutional deterioration.

1991.assessment:
Very low political violence. Democratic transition year from Panchayat
to multiparty system. 1990 movement had some violence (40+ deaths)
but 1991 was first election year — largely peaceful. No insurgency.

1992.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Democratic system functioning. CPN-UML in
opposition. CPN-M beginning underground organizing but not yet armed.
Nepal broadly peaceful.

1993.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Coalition politics but no armed conflict.
CPN-M preparing for insurgency. Some political protests but no mass
violence.

1994.assessment:
Minimal political violence. CPN-UML briefly in minority government.
No armed conflict. Nepal's pre-insurgency period — politically active
but not violent.

1995.assessment:
Minimal political violence. CPN-M submitted 40-point demand to government
in February 1996 (pre-insurgency ultimatum). No armed violence yet.
Pre-insurgency baseline.

1996.assessment:
Insurgency launched February 13, 1996 with attacks on police posts in
western Nepal. Initial violence limited — ~50 deaths. Maoists targeting
police in Rolpa, Rukum, Sindhuli. Government response beginning.
Low-intensity phase of People's War.

1997.assessment:
Insurgency expanding. Deaths increasing as Maoists and security forces
clash in more districts. Police posts increasingly targeted. Maoists
expanding from western heartland. ~150 deaths estimated.

1998.assessment:
Insurgency intensifying. Maoists attacking police, capturing weapons.
Security forces conducting operations in Rolpa, Rukum. Deaths growing.
Government deploying Armed Police Force (established 2001).
Estimated ~200 deaths.

1999.assessment:
Growing insurgency violence. Maoists expanding to new districts.
Security forces operations increasing. Civilians caught between
two forces. ~250 deaths estimated. Pattern of village-level violence.

2000.assessment:
Insurgency at medium intensity. Maoist attacks on district headquarters.
Security force operations. ~300 deaths. Civilians targeted for
collaboration with either side. Rural population under severe pressure.

2001.assessment:
Massive escalation. Royal Nepalese Army deployed in November 2001 after
State of Emergency. Army operations caused major casualties. Maoist
Holeri attack killed 14 soldiers. Total deaths jumped to ~2,000.
Highest single-year death count. Royal massacre June — separate event
(10 royal family deaths). Insurgency reached high-intensity phase.

sources.citation: UCDP Nepal; INSEC Human Rights Yearbook Nepal 2001

2002.assessment:
High-intensity conflict. Army and Maoists fighting across Nepal.
INSEC documented ~3,500 deaths (conflict-related). Maoists and
security forces conducting operations. High civilian casualties.
Highest-intensity period of the war.

2003.assessment:
Ceasefire January-August 2003 reduced deaths significantly. Ceasefire
collapsed August 2003 with Maoist resumption. Deaths for year estimated
~1,500 (less than 2002 due to ceasefire months). Continued high violence
after ceasefire collapsed.

2004.assessment:
Continued high-intensity conflict. King Gyanendra dismissing governments.
Army and Maoist operations continuing. ~1,500 deaths. Maoists blocking
highways and enforcing bandhs. Civilians severely affected.

2005.assessment:
Declining violence. Royal coup February 2005. Maoist ceasefire September
2005. Deaths ~1,000 — declining from peak. Political dynamics shifting
with king-parties-Maoist alliance forming against royal rule.

2006.assessment:
Major de-escalation. Jana Andolan II in April resulted in reinstatement
of parliament. Peace process accelerated. Comprehensive Peace Agreement
November 2006 ended formal armed conflict. Deaths dramatically declined
to ~300 for the year. End of 10-year insurgency.

2007.assessment:
Near-peace. Armed conflict essentially ended. Some residual violence
from Terai ethnic groups (JTMM and others) not covered by CPA.
Madhesi protests and some political violence in Terai. CPA-covered
conflict nearly zero.

2008.assessment:
Minimal conflict violence. CA elections April 2008. Republic declared.
Some Terai unrest. YCL (Young Communist League) extortion and violence
cases. But armed conflict essentially ended.

2009.assessment:
Very low violence. Post-conflict transition. Some incidents involving
YCL and Terai armed groups. Prachanda resigned. Political instability
but near-zero armed conflict.

2010.assessment:
Minimal armed violence. Nepal essentially post-conflict. Some criminal
gang activity and minor Terai incidents. No organized armed insurgency.
Political violence limited to political protests.

2011.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. Post-conflict integration. CA expired
without constitution. Political deadlock but peaceful. Nepal among
most peaceful in South Asia by this point.

2012.assessment:
Minimal violence. CA dissolved. Political vacuum but peaceful. Some
Terai protests. No organized armed conflict. Nepal's conflict
transformation to politics complete.

2013.assessment:
Minimal violence. Second CA elections peaceful. Constitution drafting
resuming. Nepal broadly peaceful political environment. Residual Terai
armed group activity very low level.

2014.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Constitution drafting proceeding. Nepal
at peace. Some political protests but no armed conflict.

2015.assessment:
April earthquake (9,000+ deaths — natural disaster, not political
violence). Constitution-related protests from September 2015 resulted
in ~50 deaths in Terai/Madhes protests (security force-protestor clashes).
India blockade in response to Madhes protests. Political violence
concentrated in Terai constitution protests.

sources.citation: UCDP Nepal; Human Rights Watch Nepal 2015

2016.assessment:
Declining Madhes protest violence. India blockade ended. Some residual
Terai protests. Minimal overall political violence. Nepal largely peaceful.

2017.assessment:
Minimal violence. Federal elections held peacefully. Madhes integration
improving. Nepal's political violence at lowest sustained level since
pre-insurgency era. Political competition through elections not violence.

2018.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. Nepal functioning democratic federal
state. NCP majority government. No armed conflict. Residual incidents
only. Nepal among most peaceful in South Asia.

2019.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. Nepal federal democracy stable. No armed
conflict. NCP internal tensions but no violence. Nepal peaceful.

2020.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. COVID-19 pandemic. Some lockdown-related
incidents. Political crisis (Oli dissolved parliament) but no violence.
Constitutional crisis managed peacefully through Supreme Court.

2021.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. Parliament restored. Deuba became PM.
NCP split. Political turbulence but all managed peacefully through
institutions. Nepal's post-conflict peace consolidation maintained.

2022.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. November 2022 elections held peacefully.
Coalition government formation. Nepal democratic system functioning.
No armed conflict.

2023.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. Prachanda PM. Coalition government
changes but peaceful. Nepal's political system managing instability
without violence. No armed conflict.

2024.assessment:
Near-zero political violence. Oli returned as PM July 2024. Coalition
politics continued. Nepal's democratic system managing all political
competition peacefully. No armed conflict.

2025.assessment:
Near-zero political violence continuing. Nepal at peace. No prospect
of armed conflict resumption.

1991.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Constitutional monarchy with multiparty
democracy just established (1990). No insurgency. Government controls all
75 districts. Full sovereignty maintained.

1992.assessment:
Full government control. Democratic government established. No
significant armed challenge to state authority. All districts under
government administration.

1993.assessment:
Full government control. Democratic system functioning. No armed
challenge to state authority.

1994.assessment:
Full government control. Multiple coalition governments but no
territorial challenge. State authority maintained across all districts.

1995.assessment:
Full government control. No armed insurgency yet. CPN-M was political
party preparing for insurgency. State fully controls all territory.

1996.assessment:
Primarily full government control with insurgency beginning in remote
western districts (Rolpa, Rukum). Maoists launched People's War February
1996. Initial attacks limited. Government controls major towns and most
of country. Insurgency beginning to challenge rural control.

1997.assessment:
Government control challenged in western districts. Maoists establishing
shadow government in Rolpa, Rukum, Jajarkot. Rural western Nepal partially
outside government effective control. Urban areas and eastern districts
fully controlled. Small contested areas emerging.

1998.assessment:
Growing contested areas. Maoists expanding from western base. More
districts reporting Maoist presence. Government presence only in
district headquarters in affected areas. Rural western Nepal increasingly
Maoist-controlled at night.

1999.assessment:
Insurgency expanding to mid-western hills. Maoists controlling significant
rural areas. Government restricted to district headquarters in many
western and mid-hill districts. Major contested area developing.

2000.assessment:
Maoist insurgency at significant scale. Large portions of rural Nepal
under Maoist influence or control. Government restricted to urban centers
in affected districts. Maoists attacking police posts. Multiple districts
effectively contested.

2001.assessment:
Maoists effectively control or contest majority of rural Nepal (est. 60%
of districts substantially affected). Royal massacre June 2001.
State of emergency November 2001. Army deployed. Maoists declared
People's War extended to all 75 districts. Government controlling
district headquarters but many rural areas Maoist-controlled.

2002.assessment:
Severe loss of government territorial control. Maoists controlling or
contesting most rural areas outside urban centers. Army deployed but
government controlling only district headquarters in many districts.
Royal Nepalese Army fighting but insurgency not contained.

2003.assessment:
Continued severe loss of territorial control despite army efforts.
Ceasefire in early 2003 (failed). Maoists maintaining extensive rural
control. Multiple competing authorities — government, army, and Maoist
administration in much of rural Nepal.

2004.assessment:
Continued major territorial control challenges. Army offensives not
recovering rural areas. Maoists attacking district headquarters in
some areas. King Gyanendra increasingly asserting direct control.
Insurgency not contained.

2005.assessment:
Royal coup February 2005. King took direct control. Despite military
offensive, insurgency continued. Maoists declaring ceasefire in September
2005. Rural Nepal still largely outside effective government control.
Army held urban centers; Maoists had rural areas.

2006.assessment:
Jana Andolan II (April 2006) and Comprehensive Peace Agreement (November
2006) ended insurgency. UNMIN deployed to monitor cantonment of Maoist
forces. Government control rapidly recovering. Dual authority began
transitioning to unified government. Maoists entered parliament.

2007.assessment:
Territorial control rapidly recovering. Maoist PLA in cantonments.
Government administration returning to formerly Maoist-controlled areas.
UNMIN monitoring cantonment. Peace process advancing. Government
progressively recovering full territorial control.

2008.assessment:
Government substantially recovering territorial control. Maoists integrated
into political process (won CA elections). Some areas still in dual
administration transition. Republic declared. UNMIN monitoring ongoing.
Control largely restored to government.

2009.assessment:
Substantially full government territorial control with post-conflict
integration ongoing. PLA still in cantonments but political integration
proceeding. Minor residual contested areas in remote districts.
Post-conflict Nepal primarily under government control.

2010.assessment:
Full government territorial control. PLA integration process ongoing.
UNMIN mission ended January 2011. Nepal Army absorbing Maoist combatants.
Political instability (CA deadlock) but territorial control complete.

2011.assessment:
Full government territorial control. CA expired without constitution.
PLA integration into Nepal Army completed mostly. No armed challenge
to state authority. Full sovereignty maintained.

2012.assessment:
Full government territorial control. CA dissolved. Political vacuum but
no armed challenge. Some Terai ethnic unrest (Madhes movement) but not
challenging territorial control. Security maintained.

2013.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Second CA elected. Some Terai protests.
Post-conflict integration complete. Nepal Army fully operational. No
armed challenge to state authority.

2014.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Constitution drafting. No armed
challenges to state authority. Nepal Army controls all districts.
Full sovereignty.

2015.assessment:
Full government territorial control despite earthquake. India blockade
affected normal functioning. Terai/Madhes protests against new constitution
(from September 2015 to early 2016) included blockades of border crossings.
Government maintained territorial authority but Madhes protesters
challenged practical control of Terai border areas briefly.

2016.assessment:
Full government territorial control. India blockade and Madhes protests
ended. Political negotiations with Madhes parties. Federal structure
being implemented. Full sovereignty.

2017.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Federal elections held successfully.
Federal structure being implemented. Provincial governments elected.
No armed challenges. Nepal's post-conflict territorial control complete.

2018.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Federal structure operative.
NCP majority government. Provincial and local governments functioning.
No armed challenges to state authority.

2019.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Federal structure fully operational.
NCP government stable. No armed challenges. Nepal Army maintaining
security across all 77 districts (new federal structure).

2020.assessment:
Full government territorial control despite COVID-19 and political
crisis. Oli dissolved parliament twice (ruled unconstitutional). Political
instability but no territorial challenge. China border tensions over
maps (minor). India border incident (Kalapani) not threatening control.

2021.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Parliament restored by Supreme Court.
Deuba became PM. Political instability continuing but territorial control
complete. No armed challenges.

2022.assessment:
Full government territorial control. November 2022 elections. Prachanda
coalition government formed. No armed challenges. Nepal Army fully
controlling all territory.

2023.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Coalition politics. Multiple PM
changes within coalition framework. No armed challenges to state authority.
Full sovereignty maintained.

2024.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Oli returned as PM July 2024 in
NC-CPN-UML coalition. No armed challenges. Nepal maintains full sovereignty
and territorial control across all 77 districts.

2025.assessment:
Full government territorial control continuing. No armed challenges
to state authority. Nepal stable in territorial terms.

1991.assessment:
No OBI data available for pre-2006 period. Nepal had a basic budget process
under multiparty democracy restored in 1990. Budget documents published but
limited public access and analysis capacity. No meaningful public participation
in budget process. Qualitative assessment: minimal transparency.

sources.citation: World Bank Public Financial Management Assessment Nepal (historical)

1992.assessment:
No OBI data. Multiparty system restoring administrative processes. Budget
presented to parliament. Some improvement from pre-1990 Panchayat era.
Limited civil society capacity to analyze budget. Minimal transparency.

1993.assessment:
No OBI data. Basic budget transparency under parliamentary democracy.
Budget presented annually to parliament. Public accounts committee functional.
Limited public access to budget documents. Minimal transparency.

1994.assessment:
No OBI data. Mid-term elections. Budget transparency under parliamentary
system. Annual budget presented. Limited detail in public documents.
Minimal to limited transparency range.

1995.assessment:
No OBI data. Parliamentary democracy continuing. Budget process reasonably
functional. Limited public access and analytical capacity. Assessment:
minimal to limited transparency.

1996.assessment:
No OBI data. Maoist insurgency launched February 1996. Budget process
continuing under parliamentary system. Security expenditure growing but
not fully disclosed. Limited transparency on security-related fiscal matters.

sources.citation: World Bank Nepal PFM; INSEC Nepal

1997.assessment:
No OBI data. Parliamentary budget process continuing. Defense and security
spending increasing but transparency declining for security items. Civil
society beginning to engage on budget transparency issues.

1998.assessment:
No OBI data. Budget transparency minimal under parliamentary democracy.
Emergency expenditures for counterinsurgency not fully transparent.
Political instability with multiple government changes limiting
consistent budget process improvement.

1999.assessment:
No OBI data. Parliamentary democracy. Budget process functional but limited
transparency. Security spending increasing. World Bank/IMF PRSP process
beginning to require more fiscal documentation for development assistance.

sources.citation: World Bank Nepal PFM; IMF PRSP Nepal

2000.assessment:
No OBI data. Budget transparency declining as security spending increased
without full disclosure. Emergency military expenditures not publicly
detailed. Parliament occasionally bypassed for emergency security spending.

sources.citation: World Bank Nepal PFM; Transparency International

2001.assessment:
No OBI data. Nepal Army mobilized November 2001. Emergency security expenditures
major budget item without full parliamentary oversight. Royal massacre June 2001
created political instability. Budget transparency at low point due to
security emergency.

2002.assessment:
No OBI data. Parliament dismissed October 2002. Budget process severely
compromised without legislature. Emergency executive budgeting. Scant
transparency with parliament prorogued and emergency military spending.

2003.assessment:
No OBI data. Parliament still prorogued. Ceasefire January-August 2003
provided brief relative stability. Budget process by executive ordinance.
Scant transparency continuing without legislative oversight.

2004.assessment:
No OBI data. Parliament prorogued. Budget by executive decree. Security
emergency spending dominating. Scant transparency. Royal political interventions
further weakened fiscal accountability.

2005.assessment:
No OBI data. Royal coup February 2005 suspended all democratic institutions.
Budget by royal executive decree. No parliamentary oversight. Scant transparency
at absolute minimum — emergency executive control of all public finances
without legislative accountability.

2006.assessment:
Nepal's first OBI score (IBP 2006 survey): 28/100, classified as "minimal
information." Parliament restored April 2006 (Jana Andolan II). Budget process
being normalized with peace agreement November 2006. Score reflects the
transitional state between royal executive control and restored democratic
fiscal oversight.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006; Nepal OBI score 28

2007.assessment:
No OBI survey in 2007 (IBP surveys are biennial). Interim legislature
constituted. Budget process normalizing under peace process. UNMIN
monitoring cantonment. Transparency improving from 2005-2006 low point
but still limited. Estimate: minimal to limited range.

sources.citation: IBP OBI biennial; World Bank Nepal PFM 2007

2008.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2008: 45/100, classified as "limited information" (threshold 40-60).
Significant improvement from 28 in 2006. Constituent Assembly elected April 2008,
republic declared May 2008. Budget process under new democratic framework.
Improvement reflects post-peace normalization of fiscal governance.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008; Nepal OBI score 45

2009.assessment:
No OBI survey year. Interpolated from 2008 (45) and 2010 survey.
Political deadlock over constitution continued. Budget process under
Constituent Assembly regime. Estimate: limited information range.

2010.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2010: 42/100, classified as "limited information." Slight decline
from 2008 (45). Constitutional Assembly deadlock contributing to governance
uncertainty. Budget process functional but oversight weakened by political
instability. Still significantly better than pre-2006 period.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010; Nepal OBI score 42

2011.assessment:
No OBI survey year. CA expired without constitution May 2011. Budget process
continuing under caretaker arrangement. Limited information range maintained.

2012.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2012: 40/100, classified as "limited information." Slight decline
continuing. CA dissolved May 2012 without constitution. Constitutional vacuum
affecting budget oversight. Still within limited information category.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012; Nepal OBI score 40

2013.assessment:
No OBI survey year. New CA elected November 2013. Budget process stabilizing.
Limited information range continued.

2014.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2015: 26/100, marked decline to "minimal information." Significant
deterioration from 40 (2012). Constitutional uncertainty, delayed budget
processes, and weak parliamentary oversight contributed to decline.
Note: OBI uses survey year T for the following year's report publication.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015; Nepal OBI score 26

2015.assessment:
No OBI survey year. Earthquake April 2015 and India blockade September 2015
disrupted normal budget processes. Emergency expenditures not fully transparent.
New constitution September 2015 provided new institutional framework.
Minimal information range continuing.

sources.citation: IBP OBI biennial interpolation; World Bank Nepal post-disaster

2016.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2017: 36/100 — significant recovery, back in "minimal information"
upper range. Post-earthquake reconstruction planning improved fiscal
documentation. New constitution framework being implemented. Recovery
from 2015 low point.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017; Nepal OBI score 36

2017.assessment:
No OBI survey year. First federal elections November-December 2017.
Budget process under new federal structure beginning. Estimate: minimal
to limited range as new framework being established.

2018.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2019: 46/100 — significant improvement, crossing into "limited
information" category. Federal structure bringing new accountability.
Stable Oli government (large parliamentary majority) improving governance.
MCC Compact process requiring fiscal transparency improvements. Best
score since 2008.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019; Nepal OBI score 46

2019.assessment:
No OBI survey year. Continuing federal implementation. Political crisis
beginning (Oli NCP internal divisions). Estimate: limited information range
continuing from 2018 baseline.

2020.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2021: 39/100 — back in "minimal information" range. COVID-19
emergency spending reduced transparency. Political crisis (Oli dissolved
parliament twice). Emergency health expenditures not fully disclosed.
Decline from 46 (2018) reflecting COVID + political instability.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021; Nepal OBI score 39

2021.assessment:
No OBI survey year. Political crisis resolution by Supreme Court. Post-COVID
normalization beginning. MCC Compact ratified February 2022. Estimate:
minimal to limited range.

2022.assessment:
Nepal OBI 2023: 40/100 — marginal improvement, at boundary of limited
information (40-60 range). November 2022 elections. Forex reserve crisis
and import bans reduced some fiscal transparency. Federal budget process
more institutionalized. Stable at 40 level.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2023; Nepal OBI score 40

2023.assessment:
No OBI survey year. Continuing coalition governments. Budget process stable.
Estimate: limited information range.

2024.assessment:
OBI 2025 survey results not yet published. NC-CPN-UML coalition government
stable. Budget process continuing under federal framework. Estimate based
on trajectory: limited information range.

2025.assessment: Data not available. Next OBI survey not yet conducted.
