1991.assessment:
No WJP data available pre-2008. Nepal restored multiparty democracy in 1990.
Constitution of 1990 established rule of law framework. Courts functional
but with limited independence. Laws published in Nepal Gazette but poor
public access. Judiciary establishing independence after Panchayat era.
Assessment: partial to significant gaps in legal transparency.

sources.citation: Constitution of Nepal 1990; USAID Nepal rule of law assessment

1992.assessment:
No WJP data. Constitutional governance being established. Nepal Supreme
Court increasingly asserting independence. Parliamentary legislation
being published. Limited public access to legal texts. Significant gaps
in enforcement and judicial reach in rural areas.

sources.citation: ICJ Nepal Rule of Law Report; USAID

1993.assessment:
No WJP data. Rule of law improving under multiparty democracy. Laws
published but poor public access in rural areas. Judiciary building
capacity. Significant enforcement gaps in hills and mountains. Local
administration improving but inconsistent.

1994.assessment:
No WJP data. Parliamentary democracy with reasonable rule of law framework.
Mid-term elections. Laws published. Judiciary functional. Partial transparency
with significant access gaps.

1995.assessment:
No WJP data. Stable parliamentary democracy. Constitution of 1990 providing
framework. Laws accessible through Nepal Gazette. Courts functional but
backlogs significant. Partial transparency with significant gaps.

1996.assessment:
No WJP data. Maoist insurgency launched February 1996 attacking
government presence in rural areas including courts and police posts.
Rule of law framework intact in urban areas but deteriorating in Maoist-affected
districts. Legal transparency beginning to decline in conflict areas.

1997.assessment:
No WJP data. Growing insurgency displacing government presence. Courts
and police posts attacked or abandoned in Maoist-controlled areas. Rule
of law functioning in urban centers and district headquarters only.
Difficult/arbitrary enforcement in conflict zones.

1998.assessment:
No WJP data. Insurgency intensifying. Government rule of law effectively
absent in large parts of mid-western and far-western Nepal. Emergency
security laws increasing arbitrary enforcement. Extra-judicial killings
by security forces documented. Difficult arbitrary enforcement nationwide.

sources.citation: Amnesty International Nepal 1998; INSEC Nepal human rights yearbook

1999.assessment:
No WJP data. Insurgency continuing. Government law enforcement concentrated
in district headquarters. Maoists running parallel governance in areas they
controlled including parallel courts. Dual legal system: government and
Maoist. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

2000.assessment:
No WJP data. Active insurgency at intensifying phase. Government rule of law
functioning in urban areas only. Extrajudicial killings by security forces.
Maoists enforcing own laws in controlled areas. Legal framework minimal
outside urban centers.

2001.assessment:
No WJP data. Nepal Army mobilized November 2001. Emergency security laws
invoked. Arbitrary detention and extrajudicial executions documented.
Royal massacre June 2001. Legal framework severely compromised in conflict
zones and under emergency provisions. Minimal rule by decree elements.

sources.citation: INSEC Nepal 2001; UN Nepal human rights reports

2002.assessment:
No WJP data. Parliament dismissed October 2002. Emergency executive rule.
Terrorism Prevention Ordinance invoked extensively. NHRC documented mass
disappearances and extrajudicial killings. Rule of law at low point with
parliament dissolved and emergency security laws dominant.

sources.citation: NHRC Nepal 2002; INSEC Nepal; HRW Nepal 2002

2003.assessment:
No WJP data. Ceasefire January-August 2003 provided partial relief from
extreme extrajudicial violence. Parliament still prorogued. Emergency
security laws still operative. Human rights violations by both sides
documented. Minimal rule by decree continuing.

2004.assessment:
No WJP data. Parliament prorogued. Conflict at high intensity. Emergency
security laws operative. Royal political interventions. Rule of law framework
minimal outside urban centers with rule by decree patterns.

2005.assessment:
No WJP data. Royal coup February 2005. State of emergency declared.
All democratic oversight suspended. Media censorship. Politicians imprisoned.
Royal executive decree replacing all legal processes. Worst rule of law
point: no accessible legal framework, royal decree replacing all law.

sources.citation: HRW Nepal 2005; ICJ Nepal; Amnesty International 2005

2006.assessment:
No WJP data. Jana Andolan II April 2006 restored democracy. CPA November 2006.
Parliament restored. Constitutional rule restored by end of year. Rapid
improvement from 2005 low. Rule of law transitioning back toward functional
constitutional framework. Difficult enforcement in transition.

2007.assessment:
No WJP data. Interim constitution. UNMIN monitoring. Nepal Supreme Court
active. Maoists in interim government. Impunity for conflict-era human rights
violations remaining. Legal framework transitional. Partial significant gaps.

sources.citation: INSEC Nepal 2007; ICJ Nepal transition report

2008.assessment:
WJP 2008 Nepal: 0.39 (score 0-1; converted to 39/100 per quantitative_transform).
CA elections April 2008. Republic declared May 2008. Legal framework transitional
but increasingly functional. Weak but partially functional rule of law. Courts
active including Supreme Court asserting independence.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2008; Nepal score 0.39

2009.assessment:
WJP estimated ~0.40 (WJP survey may not have included Nepal annually in early
years). Republic consolidating. Constitutional Assembly debating. Maoists in
and out of government. Courts increasingly assertive. Partial significant gaps
with improving trajectory.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index; Nepal interpolated from 2008/2010

2010.assessment:
WJP 2010 Nepal approximately 0.41. Constitutional Assembly deadlock.
Courts active. Rule of law improving gradually. Partial significant gaps
continuing — impunity for conflict-era crimes, weak local governance,
Tarai ethnic tensions.

2011.assessment:
WJP 2011 Nepal approximately 0.41. CA expired without constitution May 2011.
Constitutional vacuum affecting legal framework. Courts still functioning.
Supreme Court increasingly asserting itself against political decisions.
Partial significant gaps — constitutional uncertainty a key issue.

2012.assessment:
WJP 2012 Nepal approximately 0.42. CA dissolved May 2012. Caretaker
executive governance without parliament. Courts functioning independently.
Supreme Court rulings on political issues significant. Partial significant gaps.

2013.assessment:
WJP 2013 Nepal approximately 0.43. New CA elected November 2013.
Constitutional process reinvigorated. Judiciary functioning. Right to
Information Act 2007 improving legal transparency. Gradual improvement.
Partial gaps continuing.

2014.assessment:
WJP 2014 Nepal approximately 0.44. Constitution drafting nearing completion.
Judicial sector reform. Gradual improvement in rule of law. Most accessible,
some gaps — Nepal approaching this level with functional courts and published laws
but enforcement gaps persist.

2015.assessment:
WJP 2015 Nepal approximately 0.44. New constitution promulgated September 2015.
Earthquake April 2015 disrupted courts temporarily. India blockade September 2015.
Federal constitution framework providing new legal architecture. Stable at
similar level despite disruptions.

2016.assessment:
WJP 2016 Nepal approximately 0.46. Federal constitution implementation
beginning. New institutional framework. Judiciary increasingly robust.
Supreme Court rulings on constitutional matters carrying weight. Most
accessible with some gaps.

2017.assessment:
WJP 2017-18 Nepal approximately 0.48. Federal elections November-December 2017
completing constitutional transition. Stable democratic governance. Courts
assertive. Right to Information expanding. Most accessible with some gaps —
best rule of law period in Nepal's modern history.

2018.assessment:
WJP 2019 Nepal approximately 0.49. Stable Oli government. Federal structure
fully implemented. Provincial and local governments functioning under new
constitutional framework. MCC Compact requiring good governance improvements.
Most accessible with some gaps — improving trend.

2019.assessment:
WJP 2020 Nepal approximately 0.48. Political tensions in NCP (Oli vs Prachanda).
Rule of law stable despite political divisions. Judiciary active. Courts
beginning to adjudicate complex constitutional matters. Most accessible
with some gaps maintaining.

2020.assessment:
WJP 2021 Nepal approximately 0.47. Political crisis: Oli dissolved parliament
twice (December 2020, May 2021). Supreme Court restored parliament both times.
Critically: Supreme Court asserting rule of law over executive actions
demonstrated Nepal's improving constitutionalism. COVID disruptions to courts.

2021.assessment:
WJP 2022 Nepal approximately 0.47. Political crisis resolved. Deuba government.
MCC Compact ratified February 2022. Rule of law stable. Judiciary assertive.
Parliamentary elections November 2022. Most accessible with some gaps.

2022.assessment:
WJP 2023 Nepal approximately 0.47. Elections November 2022. Coalition
government formation. Courts active. Stable rule of law framework.
Most accessible with some gaps — consistent level maintained.

2023.assessment:
WJP 2024 Nepal approximately 0.47. Coalition governments continuing.
Stable rule of law. Nepal judiciary known for independence in South Asian
context. Most accessible with some gaps.

2024.assessment:
WJP 2024-25 Nepal approximately 0.47. NC-CPN-UML coalition. Stable governance.
Federal structure mature. Courts active. Most accessible with some gaps
continuing at consistent level.

2025.assessment:
WJP 2025 data not yet available. Based on trajectory, Nepal expected to
maintain approximately 0.47 level. Most accessible with some gaps.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index forthcoming

1991.assessment:
No RSF data available pre-2002. Nepal restored multiparty democracy and
free press in 1990. 1991 saw flowering of new independent newspapers and
FM radio stations. Freedom House rated Nepal's press as Partly Free.
Significant improvement from Panchayat-era censorship. Qualitative
assessment: satisfactory to problematic range.

sources.citation: Freedom House Press Freedom Report 1991; RSF historical

1992.assessment:
No RSF data. Multiparty press freedom. Independent newspapers and radio
proliferating. Some political pressure on media. Freedom House: Partly Free.
Problematic range: political interference, economic pressures on media.

1993.assessment:
No RSF data. Free press under parliamentary democracy. Independent
newspapers, radio (some FM), limited TV. Political pressure on media.
Problematic: self-censorship on sensitive topics (monarchy, security).

1994.assessment:
No RSF data. Active press in Kathmandu. Political pressure on journalists
covering corruption. Mid-term elections covered relatively freely.
Problematic range with some self-censorship.

1995.assessment:
No RSF data. Parliamentary democracy with active media. Independent
newspapers and radio proliferating in Kathmandu. Rural media very limited.
Some political pressure on journalists. Problematic environment.

1996.assessment:
No RSF data. Maoist insurgency launched February 1996. Initial impact
on rural journalists: Maoists threatened media not following their line.
Kathmandu press remained relatively free. Growing bifurcation of press
freedom: better in Kathmandu, deteriorating in insurgency areas.

1997.assessment:
No RSF data. Growing pressure on journalists in insurgency areas.
Maoists attacking media offices and threatening reporters. Government
also detaining journalists who covered Maoist activities. Increasing
self-censorship. Difficult environment emerging in conflict zones.

sources.citation: CPJ Nepal 1997; IFEX Nepal; Freedom House

1998.assessment:
No RSF data. Journalists attacked and detained by both security forces
and Maoists. Emergency security laws used against journalists covering
conflict. Rural media severely constrained. Kathmandu press still
relatively active but covering conflict with self-censorship.

1999.assessment:
No RSF data. Continuing difficult environment. Journalists in conflict
areas subject to threats from both sides. Government using sedition
laws against some journalists. Difficult press environment.

2000.assessment:
No RSF data. Difficult environment. Media operating under dual threat from
Maoists and security forces. Maoists bombing media offices considered
too pro-government. Police detaining journalists covering Maoist activity.
Significant chilling effect on reporting.

2001.assessment:
No RSF data. Nepal Army mobilized November 2001. Emergency security laws
invoked. State of emergency included media restrictions. Journalists detained.
Royal massacre coverage subject to restrictions. Very serious environment
emerging with emergency provisions restricting reporting.

2002.assessment:
RSF 2002 Nepal approximately rank 110/167. RSF inverted score ~40/100.
State of emergency continued through February 2002. Parliament dismissed
October 2002. Emergency laws used against journalists. Both army and
Maoists attacking reporters. Editors jailed. Very serious press environment.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2002; CPJ Nepal 2002

2003.assessment:
RSF 2003 Nepal approximately rank 109. Ceasefire January-August 2003 provided
brief improvement. Post-ceasefire renewed conflict. Journalists killed and
detained. CPJ documented multiple cases. Media in ceasefire period slightly
more active but overall very serious environment.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2003; CPJ Nepal attacks on press 2003

2004.assessment:
RSF 2004 Nepal approximately rank 128/167. Worsening. Journalists killed,
detained, beaten by both sides. Self-censorship pervasive. FM radio stations
forced to broadcast only music and no news. Very serious environment.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2004; CPJ Nepal

2005.assessment:
RSF 2005 Nepal approximately rank 138/167. Royal coup February 2005.
State of emergency. All FM stations banned from airing news. Newspapers
subject to prior censorship. Journalists arrested. Internet restricted.
Very serious to approaching no_press_freedom: systematic royal crackdown.
Worst press freedom in Nepal's post-1990 history.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2005; CPJ Nepal 2005; Amnesty International

2006.assessment:
RSF 2006 Nepal approximately rank 106/168. Significant improvement. Jana
Andolan II April 2006 restored press freedom. FM stations resumed news
broadcasting. Peace agreement November 2006. Rapid transition from 2005
censorship to restored press freedom. Still difficult: conflict continuing,
transition uncertainty.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2006; CPJ Nepal 2006

2007.assessment:
RSF 2007 Nepal approximately rank 100/169. Post-peace process media freedom
recovery. Maoists joining interim government. Journalists still facing threats
from armed groups (YCL: Young Communist League). Overall improving. Difficult
environment with some political threats to journalists remaining.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2007; CPJ Nepal

2008.assessment:
RSF 2008 Nepal approximately rank 99/173. Improving. CA elections covered
freely. Republic declared. New democratic environment. Journalists more able
to operate but continued threats from Maoist-aligned groups. Problematic:
political pressure, physical threats from armed groups, economic pressures.

2009.assessment:
RSF 2009 Nepal approximately rank 95/175. Continuing improvement. Democratic
republic consolidating. Press freedom improving. Political parties competing
without major press crackdowns. Problematic: occasional journalist attacks,
armed group threats, economic pressures.

2010.assessment:
RSF 2010 Nepal approximately rank 94/178. Stable improvement. Nepal ranks
relatively better than neighbors in South Asia (better than India, Pakistan,
Bangladesh, Sri Lanka). Active independent press. Constitutional debates
covered. Problematic environment but improving.

2011.assessment:
RSF 2011-12 Nepal approximately rank 90/179. Improving. CA expired without
constitution May 2011 but no press crackdown. Political instability without
media repression. Nepal's press diversifying with online media growing.
Problematic: journalist attacks, self-censorship on ethnic/Madhes topics.

2012.assessment:
RSF 2013 Nepal approximately rank 90/179. CA dissolved without constitution.
Press freedom maintained. Nepal maintaining relatively good South Asian ranking.
Problematic: occasional journalist violence, political pressures, self-censorship
on sensitive topics (federalism, ethnic identity).

2013.assessment:
RSF 2014 Nepal approximately rank 87/180. New CA elected November 2013.
Improving press environment. Nepal among better-performing South Asian
countries. Problematic range but approaching satisfactory. Constitutional
debate covered actively.

2014.assessment:
RSF 2015 Nepal approximately rank 83/180. Continuing good trajectory.
New constitution nearing completion. Active media scene. Nepal ranked
best in South Asia for press freedom in some indices. Problematic to
satisfactory boundary — some journalist attacks but decreasing frequency.

2015.assessment:
RSF 2016 Nepal approximately rank 84/180. New constitution September 2015
guaranteed press freedom. Earthquake coverage allowed freely. India blockade
covered actively. Some media restrictions during earthquake emergency but
not severe. Overall good press freedom environment. Problematic range.

2016.assessment:
RSF 2017 Nepal approximately rank 80/180. Good trajectory. Media Law
reforms. Nepal's press freedom among best in South Asia. Federal
constitution providing strong press freedom protections. Problematic
category still due to online media restrictions and journalist attacks.

2017.assessment:
RSF 2018 Nepal approximately rank 76/180. Continued improvement. Federal
elections November-December 2017. Active press coverage. Nepal among
top tier in South Asia. Approaching satisfactory range. Some concerns
about IT legislation with defamation potential.

2018.assessment:
RSF 2019 Nepal approximately rank 75/180. Penal Code 2017 introduced
criminal defamation provisions. Online media laws creating chilling effects.
Despite legal concerns, active independent press operating. Media diversity
high. Problematic environment but improving trend continuing.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2019; SEAPA Nepal media report

2019.assessment:
RSF 2020 Nepal approximately rank 78/180. Slight deterioration. Political
instability (NCP internal split). IT Bill with cybercrime provisions
passing. Online journalists prosecuted under criminal defamation.
Problematic: online media freedom declining while print/broadcast
freedom maintained.

2020.assessment:
RSF 2021 Nepal approximately rank 81/180. COVID-19 coverage restrictions.
Electronic Transaction Act used against journalists. Political crisis (Oli
parliament dissolution). Some self-censorship on political topics. Problematic
range maintained but digital rights declining.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2021; ARTICLE 19 Nepal

2021.assessment:
RSF 2022 Nepal approximately rank 82/180. Political crisis resolution.
Electronic Transaction Act cases continuing against online journalists.
Print and broadcast media active. Nepal still among better-ranked South
Asian countries. Problematic range with digital media concerns.

2022.assessment:
RSF 2023 Nepal approximately rank 86/180. Slight decline. Mass Media Act
with concerning provisions. Online media restrictions. Parliamentary elections
November 2022 covered relatively freely. Problematic environment.

2023.assessment:
RSF 2024 Nepal approximately rank 88/180. Continuing concern about
online media laws. Print and broadcast media active. Nepal maintaining
Partly Free status. Problematic: digital rights restrictions and
occasional journalist intimidation.

2024.assessment:
RSF 2024-25 Nepal approximately rank 90/180. NC-CPN-UML coalition. Media
freedom maintained for traditional press. Online media laws still concerning.
Nepal remains among better-ranked South Asian countries. Problematic
environment stable.

2025.assessment:
RSF 2025 data not yet published. Based on trajectory, Nepal expected
to remain in problematic range with concerns about online media restrictions.

1991.assessment:
No SCI data available (SCI started 2004; discontinued 2020). Nepal's
statistical system in early 1990s was rudimentary. CBS (Central Bureau
of Statistics) established but with limited capacity. Census conducted 1991.
Basic national accounts but limited sectoral data. Pre-computerized systems.
Assessment: very poor statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Nepal statistical capacity assessment (historical)

1992.assessment:
No SCI data. Basic statistical system post-census 1991. National accounts
data available but with significant limitations. Limited sectoral data.
Very poor statistical capacity.

1993.assessment:
No SCI data. Gradually improving with donor assistance. World Bank and
UNDP supporting CBS capacity development. National living standards survey
beginning. Very poor to poor range.

1994.assessment:
No SCI data. Nepal Living Standards Survey 1994 (NLSS I) conducted —
first major household survey. Improving statistical capacity but still weak.
Very poor range with some improvement from donor support.

1995.assessment:
No SCI data. Post-NLSS I capacity building. CBS expanding with donor
assistance. National accounts improving. Maoist insurgency not yet
affecting statistical operations. Improving slowly from very poor baseline.

1996.assessment:
No SCI data. Maoist insurgency launched February 1996 but not yet affecting
central statistical operations. Kathmandu-based CBS continuing normal work.
Field data collection in rural areas beginning to be affected. Poor range.

sources.citation: CBS Nepal; World Bank Nepal statistical capacity

1997.assessment:
No SCI data. Insurgency expanding. CBS Kathmandu operations continuing.
Field data collection in insurgency-affected districts severely limited.
National accounts based on extrapolations for conflict areas. Poor range.

1998.assessment:
No SCI data. Active insurgency limiting rural field operations. Nepal
Living Standards Survey (NLSS II) planned for 2002-03. Basic sectoral
data produced but with significant coverage gaps from conflict areas.
Poor statistical capacity.

1999.assessment:
No SCI data. Continuing poor capacity with conflict-affected data gaps.
Some WB and ADB supported improvements in national accounts methodology.
Population census planned for 2001. Poor range.

sources.citation: CBS Nepal; ADB Nepal statistical support

2000.assessment:
No SCI data. Active insurgency. Statistical operations continuing but
with major gaps in conflict areas. Pre-census preparations for 2001 census.
Poor statistical capacity.

2001.assessment:
No SCI data. Population census 2001 conducted despite insurgency —
significant achievement. Some districts had limited coverage due to security.
State of emergency November 2001 limited statistical work. Overall poor capacity
but census represents improvement.

2002.assessment:
No SCI data. Active insurgency at peak. Parliament dismissed October 2002.
Statistical operations severely disrupted in rural areas. CBS Kathmandu
continuing basic operations. Emergency governance not conducive to statistical
improvement. Poor capacity.

2003.assessment:
No SCI data. Nepal Living Standards Survey II (NLSS II) conducted 2003-04 —
major data collection achievement despite conflict. Donor support maintaining
CBS capacity despite political instability. Poor range but with donor-assisted
capacity building continuing.

2004.assessment:
WB SCI 2004 Nepal: 46.7/100 (first year of SCI data). Classified as moderate
capacity. Score reflects donor-assisted improvements to CBS despite ongoing
insurgency. NLSS II just completed. Active insurgency limiting field data
collection but Kathmandu-based statistical systems improving. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2004; Nepal 46.7

2005.assessment:
WB SCI 2005 Nepal: 46.7/100. Royal coup February 2005. Despite political
crisis, CBS maintained operations in Kathmandu. Statistical operations not
the primary target of political disruption. Moderate capacity maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2005; Nepal 46.7

2006.assessment:
WB SCI 2006 Nepal: approximately 50.0/100. Democracy restored April 2006.
Peace agreement November 2006. Statistical capacity improving with post-conflict
normalization. NLSS data available for policy planning. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2006; Nepal ~50.0

2007.assessment:
WB SCI 2007 Nepal: approximately 54.4/100. Post-peace process improvement.
CBS expanding field data collection as security normalized. Interim government
supporting statistical operations. Improving moderately toward upper moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2007; Nepal ~54.4

2008.assessment:
WB SCI 2008 Nepal: approximately 56.7/100. Republic declared May 2008.
Constitutional Assembly elections. Democratic governance improving statistical
infrastructure. Moderate capacity continuing to improve.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2008; Nepal ~56.7

2009.assessment:
WB SCI 2009 Nepal: approximately 57.8/100. Continuing improvement. CBS
expanding coverage. Nepal Living Standards Survey planning. Moderate capacity
with steady improvement trajectory.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2009; Nepal ~57.8

2010.assessment:
WB SCI 2010 Nepal: approximately 61.1/100. Crossed into good range (60+).
Post-conflict capacity building showing results. CBS improving methodology.
National accounts quality improving. Annual surveys conducted. First time
Nepal reaches good statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2010; Nepal ~61.1

2011.assessment:
WB SCI 2011 Nepal: approximately 62.2/100. Good range. CA expired without
constitution May 2011 but CBS operations unaffected. Statistical systems
maturing. Population census planned for 2011 (conducted 2011). Good capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2011; CBS Nepal Census 2011

2012.assessment:
WB SCI 2012 Nepal: approximately 65.6/100. Good range improving. Nepal
Living Standards Survey III (NLSS III) 2010-11 data available. Better
household data. Post-census 2011 data quality improvements. Good capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2012; CBS NLSS III

2013.assessment:
WB SCI 2013 Nepal: approximately 67.8/100. Continuing good trajectory.
New CA elected November 2013. CBS operations unaffected by political
instability. International donor support for statistical capacity maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2013; Nepal ~67.8

2014.assessment:
WB SCI 2014 Nepal: approximately 68.9/100. Good and improving. Constitution
drafting. CBS receiving increased support for post-constitution statistical
planning. National accounts improving. Good capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2014; Nepal ~68.9

2015.assessment:
WB SCI 2015 Nepal: approximately 71.1/100. Good range improving further.
Gorkha earthquake April 2015 damaged some statistical infrastructure but
CBS quickly resumed operations. Post-earthquake recovery data needs driving
improvements. New constitution September 2015 establishing federal statistical
framework requirements.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2015; Nepal ~71.1

2016.assessment:
WB SCI 2016 Nepal: approximately 72.2/100. Good capacity. Federal constitution
implementation requires new statistical frameworks for provinces. CBS planning
expansion to support federal system. Post-earthquake data for reconstruction
planning. Good capacity continuing to improve.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2016; Nepal ~72.2

2017.assessment:
WB SCI 2017 Nepal: approximately 73.3/100. Good capacity, near top of range.
Federal elections November-December 2017. CBS expanding to federal system.
National accounts methodology improvements. High quality for South Asia.
Nepal Statistical Development Strategy being implemented.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2017; Nepal ~73.3

2018.assessment:
WB SCI 2018 Nepal: approximately 73.3/100. Good capacity maintained at
high South Asian level. Stable government under Oli. CBS operating well.
Nepal NHDR (National Human Development Report) regularly published.
Good statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2018; Nepal ~73.3

2019.assessment:
WB SCI 2019 Nepal: approximately 74.4/100. Near top of good range.
Nepal Living Standards Survey V planned. CBS capacity at highest ever.
Federal statistical system being established. Nepal among better South
Asian performers for statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2019; Nepal ~74.4

2020.assessment:
WB SCI 2020 Nepal: approximately 74.4/100 (FINAL SCI DATA YEAR — series
discontinued after 2020). COVID-19 disrupted some statistical operations
but CBS maintained core functions. Political crisis (Oli parliament dissolution)
did not affect statistical operations significantly. Good capacity maintained
at near-peak level.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicators 2020 (final year); Nepal ~74.4

2021.assessment:
No WB SCI data (series discontinued). Political crisis resolution. CBS
operations continuing. Post-COVID recovery. Based on institutional trajectory
assessment: good capacity maintained. Nepal's statistical infrastructure
remains strongest since early 2010s. Good capacity estimated.

sources.citation: CBS Nepal 2021 publications; World Bank alternative SPI

2022.assessment:
No WB SCI data. Nepal's CBS operations continuing. Parliamentary elections
November 2022. National Population Census completed 2021. Federal statistical
system functioning. Good capacity estimated based on institutional continuity.

sources.citation: CBS Nepal Census 2021; World Bank Nepal

2023.assessment:
No WB SCI data. CBS operations continuing under coalition government.
Federal statistical system maturing. Good capacity estimated based on
prior trajectory and institutional continuity.

2024.assessment:
No WB SCI data. NC-CPN-UML coalition. CBS operations continuing.
Nepal's federal statistical framework established. Good capacity
maintained at level consistent with late SCI period (74+ range
equivalent based on institutional assessment).

2025.assessment:
No WB SCI data. Good capacity expected to continue based on institutional
trajectory.
